Key Interval Description hourly 3600s Once every hour twicedaily 43200s Twice per day (12h) daily 86400s Once per day weekly 604800s Once per week

You can add custom schedules using the cron_schedules filter.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How-To: Using Cron — Practical guide for scheduling tasks.
  • Hooks Class — Reference for the hook system used by Cron.

docs.title: Date Class
docs.text: The Date class provides a suite of utilities for handling timezones, date formatting, localized date strings, and relative timestamps. It ensures that dates are consistently displayed according to the site's global configuration.

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration & Properties
docs.text: The class initializes its temporal settings based on the system options during boot:

docs.title: Date::$timezone
docs.text:
• Type: string
  • Description: The default timezone (e.g., 'Asia/Jakarta'). Falls back to 'UTC' if unconfigured. This is set globally via date_default_timezone_set() inside the constructor.

docs.title: Date::format(string $date, string $format = '')
docs.text:
Formats a date string into a specific pattern, adjusted to the site's configured $timezone.

Parameter Type Default Description $date string ✅ Any valid PHP date string (e.g., from DB). If empty, evaluates to current time. $format string 'j F Y H:i A T' Standard PHP date() format pattern (e.g., d/m/Y).

Example: 

echo Date::format('2024-03-29 10:00:00', 'd/m/Y');
// Outputs: 29/03/2024 (adjusted to site timezone)

echo Date::format('2024-03-29 10:00:00');
// Outputs: 29 March 2024 10:00 AM WIB

docs.title: Date::local(string $date, string $format = '')
docs.text:
Returns a localized date string using the PHP IntlDateFormatter extension, respecting the site's country_id locale.

[!WARNING] Unlike Date::format, this method uses ICU Date Field Symbols, not standard PHP date strings. Also, this method automatically appends the timezone abbreviation (e.g., "WIB", "EST") to the end of the returned string.

  • Note: Requires the intl PHP extension to be enabled on your server.
  • Format System: ICU Date Field Symbol Table.
  • Default format: 'MMMM d, Y H:m:s'

Example: 

// If Options::v('country_id') is set to 'id_ID' (Indonesian Locale)
echo Date::local('2024-03-29', 'EEEE, d MMMM Y');
// Outputs: Jumat, 29 Maret 2024 WIB

docs.title: Date::rel(string $ptime)
docs.text:
Returns a human-readable relative time string comparing the target timestamp to the current time.

echo Date::rel('2024-03-29 08:00:00');
// Outputs: 5 hours ago

Supported conversions: seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months, and years.

docs.title: Date::monthName(int $month)
docs.text:
Returns the full English name of a month given its numeric value (1-12).

echo Date::monthName(3); 
// Outputs: March

docs.title: Date::timeZone()
docs.text: Returns an associative array of all available world timezones filtered to major geographic regions (America, Asia, Europe, Pacific, etc.). It groups closely related identifiers into a comma-separated string for concise UI rendering.

docs.title: Date::optTimeZone(string $val = '')
docs.text:
Generates a raw string of HTML <option> tags containing the list of all world timezones.

Parameter Type Description $val string The timezone string to be marked as SELECTED (e.g. 'Asia/Jakarta').

docs.title: Date::countryList()
docs.text: Returns an exhaustive associative array mapping ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 country codes to their full English names. (Example: ['ID' => 'Indonesia', 'US' => 'United States'])

docs.title: Date::optCountry(string $val = '')
docs.text:
Generates HTML <option> tags for all countries in the countryList().

echo '<select name="country" class="form-control">' . Date::optCountry('ID') . '</select>';

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Options Class — Where timezone and country_id preferences are saved natively.
  • PHP DateTime Class — Underlying engine for the Date class.
  • PHP IntlDateFormatter — Engine required for Date::local().

docs.title: DB Class
docs.text:
The Db class is the foundational database abstraction layer for GeniXCMS. It uses PDO to handle secure, prepared-statement queries across multiple database drivers (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite).

[!IMPORTANT] Since version 2.0.0, it is highly recommended to use the Query Builder or the ORM / Model layer for standard data operations. They provide a more maintainable, expressive, and safer interface than direct Db calls.

docs.title: Connection
docs.text: Database credentials are automatically loaded from inc/config/config.php. The connection is established during system initialization.

docs.title: Connect Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::connect($dbhost, $dbuser, $dbpass, $dbname, $driver);

Manually establishes a database connection. Usually called automatically.

docs.title: Query Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::query(string $query, array $params = []);

Executes a SQL query. It is highly recommended to use the $params array for automatic escaping (prepared-statement style).

Db::query("UPDATE `users` SET `status` = '1' WHERE `id` = ?", [5]);

docs.title: Result Method
docs.text:
Usage: $data = Db::result(string $query, array $params = []);

Executes a SELECT query and returns an array of result objects (stdClass). This method supports Memcached if enabled in configuration.

$users = Db::result("SELECT * FROM `user` WHERE `group` = ?", [0]);
foreach ($users as $u) {
    echo $u->userid;
}

docs.title: Insert Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::insert(array $vars); or Db::insert(string $query);

Inserts a new record into a table.

docs.title: Array Mode (Recommended)
docs.text:
$data = array(
    'table' => 'posts',
    'key'   => array(
        'title'   => 'My Post',
        'content' => 'Hello World'
    )
);
Db::insert($data);

docs.title: Update Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::update(array $vars); or Db::update(string $query);

Updates existing records.

docs.title: Array Mode (Recommended)
docs.text:
$data = array(
    'table' => 'posts',
    'id'    => 12, // The record ID
    'key'   => array(
        'title' => 'Updated Title'
    )
);
Db::update($data);

docs.title: Delete Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::delete(array $vars);

Deletes records from a table.

$data = array(
    'table' => 'posts',
    'where' => array('id' => 1)
);
Db::delete($data);

docs.title: Quote Identifier
docs.text:
Usage: Db::quoteIdentifier(string $identifier);

Quotes table or column names according to the active database driver (e.g., backticks for MySQL, double quotes for PostgreSQL/SQLite).

docs.title: Close Connection
docs.text:
Usage: Db::close();

Closes the active PDO connection.

docs.title: Helper Properties
docs.text:
• Db::$num_rows: Stores the number of rows affected/returned by the last query.
  • Db::$last_id: Stores the ID generated by the last INSERT query.
  • Db::$pdo: Access the raw PDO instance directly.

docs.title: Escape Method
docs.text:
Usage: Db::escape(string $string);

Deprecated. Returns a string safe for SQL insertion. Note: Manual escaping is unnecessary when using the $params argument in query() or result().

docs.title: DummyAjax Class
docs.text: The DummyAjax class is a template and test utility for the GeniXCMS AJAX system. It serves as a simple implementation example for developers wanting to create their own AJAX endpoints.

docs.title: index()
docs.text:
The default action for the dummy AJAX service.

  • Endpoint: index.php?ajax=dummy&token=TOKEN
  • Response: Returns a JSON object with a success message.

docs.title: hello()
docs.text:
A custom action that demonstrates how to handle URL parameters.

  • Endpoint: index.php?ajax=dummy&action=hello&name=YOUR_NAME&token=TOKEN
  • URL Parameters:
    • name (string): The name to greet (default: 'World').
  • Response: Returns a personalized greeting message.

docs.title: 💡 Usage Example
docs.text:
To test the GeniXCMS AJAX dispatcher, you can call the dummy service using fetch or jQuery:

// Example using fetch
fetch('index.php?ajax=dummy&action=hello&name=GeniXCMS&token=' + GX_TOKEN)
    .then(response => response.json())
    .then(data => console.log(data.message)); // Outputs: "Hello, GeniXCMS! This is a custom AJAX action."

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ajax Class — How the dispatcher routes to these methods.
  • Token Class — Generating tokens for the request.

docs.title: Editor Class API
docs.text: The Editor class is the central coordination point for WYSIWYG editor engines in GeniXCMS. It manages the registration of editor types and handles the conditional loading of CSS/JS assets based on the user's active editor preference.

docs.title: init()
docs.text:
Bootstraps the editor system. This method is called during the core boot sequence. It:

  1. Registers the built-in editors (summernote, editorjs).
  2. Applies the editor_type_options filter to allow modules to add or modify editors.
  3. Detects the active editor from system options and executes its registration callback.
public static function init()

docs.title: register()
docs.text:
Registers a new editor engine with the system.

public static function register(string $id, string $name, callable $callback)
  • $id: A unique slug for the editor (e.g., 'gxeditor', 'tinymce').
  • $name: The display name shown in selecting the editor in System Settings.
  • $callback: The function to execute if this editor is active. This callback should handle all Asset registration and enqueuing.

docs.title: getEditors()
docs.text:
Retrieves a list of all registered editor engines.

public static function getEditors() : array
- Returns: An associative array [id => name].

docs.title: Internal Engine Callbacks
docs.text: The Editor class contains built-in methods used as callbacks for core engines. These demonstrate how to use the Asset class for editor setup:

docs.title: summernote()
docs.text:
The callback for the Summernote Classic editor. It:

  • Registers Summernote CSS/JS from CDN.
  • Injects a "raw" initialization script that handles bulk textarea activation and elFinder image uploads.

docs.title: editorjs()
docs.text:
The callback for the Editor.js block editor. It:

  • Registers core Editor.js and various tool plugins (Header, List, Image, etc.).
  • Injects a complex "raw" handler that manages the conversion between block JSON and GeniXCMS compatible HTML during form submission.

docs.title: editor_type_options (Filter)
docs.text:
Filters the available editor list. Modules can add their own editors to this list or remove default ones.

Example: 

Hooks::filter('editor_type_options', function($options) {
    $options['my_mod_editor'] = 'My Module Editor';
    return $options;
});

docs.title: Example: Custom Editor Registration
docs.text:
To register a custom editor from a module:

// In MyModule::init()
Editor::register('my_editor', 'My Cool Editor', function() {
    // 1. Register assets
    Asset::register('my-editor-js', 'js', Site::$url . '/assets/js/my-editor.js');

    // 2. Enqueue assets
    Asset::enqueue('my-editor-js');

    // 3. Inject init script
    Asset::load('my-editor-init', 'raw', '<script>/* init logic */</script>');
});

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How to Use the Editor
  • GxEditor Documentation
  • Asset Class API

docs.title: ElfinderAjax Class
docs.text: The ElfinderAjax class provides a secure connector for the elFinder file manager integration within the GeniXCMS dashboard. It manages file access permissions, automatic thumbnail generation, image resizing, and multi-format support (including WebP/AVIF conversion).

docs.title: index($param = null)
docs.text:
The primary endpoint for all elFinder file management operations. It initializes the elFinder connector, sets root paths, and handles file uploads.

  • Endpoint: index.php?ajax=elfinder&token=TOKEN
  • Actions: Handles all standard elFinder commands (open, upload, mkdir, rename, delete, etc.).

Workflow:

  1. Authentication: Verifies the system token and checks if the user has a minimum access level (access level 2 for media usage).
  2. Access Control: Implements a callback (access) to block access to hidden files (starting with .).
  3. Auto-Sorting: When auto_sort is passed in the URL, uploads are automatically sorted into images/, videos/, or audios/ subdirectories based on their extension.
  4. Post-Upload Processing:
    • Resizing: Automatically resizes images if media_autoresize_image is enabled in options.
    • Format Conversion: Automatically converts uploaded images to WebP if media_autogenerate_webp is enabled.
    • Path Normalization: Cleans up URLs to ensure consistent asset links.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Permissions
docs.text:
• Admin Access: Super-admins (access(0)) have full access to the assets/ directory and can manage system files.
  • Normal User Access: Users with access(1) or access(2) are restricted to the assets/media/ directory.
  • Upload Restrictions: Only allowed MIME types (images, audio, video, text) can be uploaded. Dangerous application types are strictly denied.
  • Sanitizer & Normalizer: All filenames are automatically sanitized and normalized to prevent path traversal or special character issues.

docs.title: 🎨 Asset Storage Backends
docs.text:
While the default is LocalFileSystem, the class is architected to support multiple storage backends as configured in the system options (media_storage_backend).

  • Thumbnail Path: Configures a dedicated cache directory for file manager thumbnails (assets/cache/thumbs/).

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ajax Class — The main AJAX dispatcher.
  • Image Class — For automated resizing and WebP conversion.
  • Options Class — How file manager behavior is configured globally.

docs.title: Files Class
docs.text: The Files class provides essential utilities for filesystem management, including recursive directory deletion, security scanning of file contents, and remote URL verification.

docs.title: delTree()
docs.text:
Recursively deletes a directory and all of its contents (files and subdirectories).

public static function delTree(string $dir) : bool
  • Warning: This action is IRREVERSIBLE. Always validate the path before calling.
  • Returns: true on success, false on failure.

docs.title: isClean()
docs.text:
Scans the first 9KB of a file for potentially malicious code patterns, PHP tags, or suspicious scripts. It supports both local and remote files.

public static function isClean(string $file) : bool
  • Returns: true if no suspicious patterns are found.
  • Security Check Highlights:
    • PHP Execution: Blocks eval, system, base64_decode, and exec.
    • Tags: Blocks <?php, <script>, and <iframe>.
    • Protocols: Blocks javascript:, vbscript:.
    • CSS Attacks: Blocks expression() and behaviour().

docs.title: isRemote()
docs.text:
Detects if a given path is a remote URL.

public static function isRemote(string $path) : bool

docs.title: remoteExist()
docs.text:
Verify if a remote URL returns an HTTP 200 status code using CURL.

public static function remoteExist(string $url) : bool

docs.title: elfinderLib()
docs.text:
[!WARNING] DEPRECATED in 2.0.0.

elFinder assets are now automatically managed and enqueued via the Asset Class during Asset::init().

docs.title: Usage in GeniXCMS
docs.text: The Files::isClean() method is a critical part of the GeniXCMS security layer. It is used internally by the Image Class before performing image manipulations (thumbFly) to prevent "Polyglot" file attacks where PHP code is hidden inside image headers.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Asset Class API — How elFinder assets are now handled.
  • Upload Class API — Handling file uploads.
  • Secure Thumbnails — Image manipulation security.

docs.title: Go API Service Integration
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.4.0 introduces a Hybrid Backend Architecture that allows high-performance read operations via a dedicated Go microservice. This service is designed to handle intensive GET requests while offloading load from the primary PHP engine, while still allowing PHP to "decorate" the data for a rich UI experience.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Go API Service acts as a high-speed read-only accelerator for the GeniXCMS ecosystem. It supports:

  • Fast Execution: Compiled binary performance (query times < 10ms).
  • Hybrid Rendering: Go handles massive raw data fetching, while PHP "decorates" it with HTML badges, actions, and formatting before sending to the browser.
  • Universal Dynamic Engine (PnP): Automatically serves any database table via mapping without extra Go code.
  • Support Core Handlers:
    • Posts: Listing and single view with URL normalization.
    • Categories: Taxonomies and hierarchy.
    • Tags: High-speed autocomplete for post tags.
    • Stats: Unified dashboard analytics (counts).
    • Version: Direct system version retrieval.
    • Search: High-performance text searching across posts.
    • Updates: System, module, and theme update checks with marketplace synchronization.
    • User: Admin user listing with role and status filtering.
    • Nixomers (Orders): High-speed Order listing with SQL JOIN support.
  • Seamless Switching: Can be toggled on/off from the Admin Panel.
  • Smart Fallback: Automatically reverts to PHP if the Go service is down or returns an error.

docs.title: 1. Go Service Environment (.env)
docs.text:
You must configure the .env file inside the go-service directory:

# Server Configuration
PORT=4544
GIN_MODE=release

# Database Configuration
DB_DRIVER=sqlite3  # alternatives: mysql, postgres
DB_HOST=127.0.0.1
DB_PORT=3306
DB_USER=root
DB_PASS=password
DB_NAME=genixcms
DB_FILE=./genixcms2.db  # For sqlite3
DB_PREFIX=              # Table prefix if any

# Authorization
GX_SECRET=your_super_secret_handshake_key

docs.title: 2. Admin Panel Settings
docs.text:
Navigate to Settings → API Service in your GeniXCMS dashboard:

  • Active Driver: Select External Go Service.
  • Service Endpoint URL: The address where your Go binary is running (e.g., http://localhost:4544).
  • Shared Authorization Secret: Must match the GX_SECRET in your .env file.
  • Auto PHP Fallback: Recommended. Reverts to standard API if Go is unreachable.

docs.title: Universal Dynamic Resource Engine (Plug n Play)
docs.text: GeniXCMS Go Service includes a powerful dynamic handler that automatically maps URL parameters to database tables. This enables instant high-performance read support for all third-party modules.

docs.title: Resource Mapping
docs.text:
Requests are mapped as follows:

  • GET /ajax/my_table → SELECT * FROM my_table
  • GET /ajax/my_table/5 → SELECT * FROM my_table WHERE id = 5

docs.title: Feature: Custom Table Mapping (PnP Hook)
docs.text:
If your AJAX resource name (e.g., inventory) is different from your database table (e.g., nix_inventory), you can map it dynamically in your PHP module without changing Go code:

Hooks::attach('go_proxy_args', function($args) {
    if ($args['resource'] === 'inventory') {
        $args['table'] = 'nix_inventory'; // Tell Go to query this table
    }
    return $args;
});

docs.title: Feature: Generic MapScan
docs.text: The dynamic engine now uses Generic MapScan. This ensures Go can safely fetch data from any table structure (even with custom columns) without needing re-compilation.

docs.title: Technical Concept: Hybrid Rendering
docs.text:
This is the key to maintaining a "Premium" look while having "Go" speed.

  1. Go Service handles the "Massive" work: Joining tables, filtering thousands of rows, and returning raw JSON.
  2. PHP Bridge identifies the response and "Decorates" it. For example, converting a status string into a colorful Bootstrap Badge or generating a Dropdown Action Menu.

docs.title: Security Restrictions (Blacklist & Whitelist)
docs.text:
To prevent data leakage, GeniXCMS uses a dual-layer security approach:

  1. Explicit Whitelist: Administrators must list allowed database tables in the API Settings dashboard.
  2. Requests to tables NOT in the whitelist return 403 Forbidden.

  3. The Go service automatically syncs with this whitelist via the handshake.

  4. Mandatory Blacklist: The following tables are Hard-Blacklisted in the Go source code and can NEVER be accessed:

  5. users, options, permissions, configs, sessions.

docs.title: AJAX-Specific Handlers
docs.text: The Go service provides dedicated AJAX endpoints optimized for admin dashboard operations:

docs.title: Updates Handler
docs.text:
• Endpoint: GET /ajax/updates
  • Purpose: Fetches and compares version information for core, installed modules, and themes against the marketplace.
  • Response: JSON with core version status, module/theme update availability, and download links.
  • Fallback: If marketplace API is unreachable, returns local version data without update information.
  • Performance: Scans filesystem for module/theme metadata once per request, cached by browser.

docs.title: User Handler
docs.text:
• Endpoint: GET /ajax/user/list_users
  • Query Parameters:
  • offset: Pagination offset (default: 0)
  • num: Records per page (default: 10)
  • q: Search query for username/email
  • group: Filter by user group/role
  • status: Filter by user status (0=inactive, 1=active)
  • Response: JSON with paginated user list, total count, and metadata.
  • Features: SQL JOINs with user_detail table to retrieve country info; role-based filtering; search support.
  • Performance: O(n) query with indexed lookups on userid/email.

docs.title: Development Case Study: Nixomers Orders
docs.text: Imagine you want a high-performance order list with payment status.

docs.title: 1. High Speed Query (Go)
docs.text: Go uses a specialized handler that performs a LEFT JOIN between nix_orders and nix_transactions to get the latest payment status instantly.

docs.title: 2. Rich UI Decoration (PHP)
docs.text:
Instead of sending raw JSON to the browser, the PHP Bridge iterates through the data and adds formatting:

// In Ajax.class.php
if ($action === 'list_orders' && $resource === 'nixomers') {
    $raw = json_decode($response, true);
    foreach ($raw['data'] as &$row) {
        $currency = Options::v('nixomers_currency') ?: 'IDR';
        $row['total_formatted'] = $currency . " " . number_format($row['total'], 0);
        // Add a badge based on payment_status from Go
        $badge = ($row['payment_status'] === 'paid') ? 'success' : 'warning';
        $row['status_badge'] = "<span class='badge bg-$badge'>$row[payment_status]</span>";
    }
    $response = json_encode($raw);
}

docs.title: Usage inside PHP (Server-side)
docs.text:
Modules can also fetch data from Go for server-side processing:

$url = Options::v('go_service_url') . '/ajax/nix_products/5';
$headers = [
    'X-GX-Secret: ' . Options::v('go_service_secret'),
    'X-GX-Table: nix_products', // Force target table
    'X-GX-Proxy: PHP-Module'
];

$response = Http::get($url, [], $headers);
$data = json_decode($response, true);

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
1. Build the Binary: 
    cd go-service
go build -o genix-api ./cmd/main.go
  2. Run as a Service (Recommended via PM2 or NSSM): 
    pm2 start ./genix-api --name "genix-hybrid-api"

docs.title: Routing Logic
docs.text:
When the Go Backend is active:

  • GET Requests: Proxied to Go if the resource is in the Whitelist.
  • POST/PUT/DELETE: Always handled by PHP to ensure Data Integrity and CSRF/Session security.
  • Fallback: If Go returns a 404 or is unreachable, the system automatically runs the local PHP Ajax class.

docs.title: GxMain Class
docs.text: The GxMain class is the primary entry point handler of the GeniXCMS application. It is responsible for orchestrating the boot sequence, determining whether the system is installed, and dispatching the user to the correct controller layer (Frontend, Backend, or Installation).

docs.title: ⚡ Application Lifecycle
docs.text:
The lifecycle of a GeniXCMS request typically starts in the root index.php, which instantiates GxMain:

  1. Instantiation: Checks if inc/config/config.php exists.
    • If Yes: Initializes the System class.
    • If No: Redirects to the install() method.
  2. Dispatching: Based on the requested URL, one of the following methods is called.

docs.title: GxMain::__construct()
docs.text:
The constructor acts as the system's "Pre-flight" check.

  • If System::existConf() is true, it bootstraps the application core.
  • If false, it immediately halts regular execution and starts the installation wizard.

docs.title: GxMain::index()
docs.text:
Triggers the Frontend controller layer.

  • Loads the theme defined in the site options.
  • Delegates request handling to Control::frontend().

docs.title: GxMain::admin()
docs.text:
Handles all requests directed towards the administrative dashboard.

  • Security Checks:
    • Calls User::secure() to ensure the user is logged in.
    • Verifies that the user has an access level of 4 (Contributor) or higher to view the dashboard.
  • Access Denied: If the user lacks permissions, it displays the noaccess error page wrapped in the admin header/footer.
  • Success: Delegates to Control::handler('backend').

docs.title: GxMain::install()
docs.text:
Initializes the standalone installation process.

  • Starts a fresh session.
  • Loads the specialized install theme (header.php, footer.php).
  • Delegates to Control::handler('install').

docs.title: 📂 Implementation Details
docs.text:
In a typical index.php, the class is used as follows:

$v = new GxMain();

if (isset($_GET['admin'])) {
    $v->admin();
} else {
    $v->index();
}

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• System Class — The core system initializer called by GxMain.
  • Control Class — The dispatcher that GxMain hands off to.
  • User Class — Used for session security in the admin() method.

docs.title: Hooks & Event-Driven Architecture Class
docs.text: The Hooks class implements the core event-driven architecture of GeniXCMS. It allows modules and themes to "hook" into system processes by attaching functions to specific execution points (Actions) or by intercepting and modifying data (Filters) before it is processed or displayed.

docs.title: ⚡ Core Operational Concepts
docs.text: GeniXCMS follows the standard Mediator Pattern for event handling.

docs.title: 🛡️ Actions (Hooks::run)
docs.text:
Execution points where you can perform a task without returning data.

  • Example: Sending an email notification after a user registers.
  • Trigger: Hooks::run('user_register_action', $user_id);

docs.title: 🧪 Filters (Hooks::filter)
docs.text:
Points where you can modify a piece of data.

  • Example: Translating or sanitizing post content before rendering.
  • Trigger: $content = Hooks::filter('post_content_filter', $content);
  • Smarter Filtering: Since version 2.0.0, if a filter is called with a single argument, the result is automatically unwrapped from its single-element array, making filter logic cleaner.

docs.title: Hooks::attach(string $name, callable $callback)
docs.text:
Registers a function to be executed when a specific hook is triggered.

// In your module's function.php
Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', 'my_module_script_loader');

function my_module_script_loader() {
    echo '<script src="..."></script>';
}

docs.title: 📝 Implementation Workflow (Standard APIs)
docs.text: Method Role Requirement attach($name, $fn) Subscribe Attaches a listener to an event. run($name, ...$args) Notify Executes all listeners for an Action. filter($name, $data) Modify Passes data through listeners for a Filter.

docs.title: 🏹 High-Impact System Hooks
docs.text:
GeniXCMS provides dozens of built-in hooks to modify system behavior:

  • init: Triggered after the system bootstrap is complete.
  • header_load_meta: Injects custom meta-tags into the <head> section.
  • post_content_filter: Modifies the HTML body of a post before rendering.
  • user_login_action: Triggered immediately after a successful authentication.
  • admin_page_top_action: Injects custom UI components at the top of admin screens.

docs.title: 🛠️ Developer Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Prefixing: Always prefix your callback functions (e.g., my_mod_handle_auth) to prevent global namespace collisions with other modules.
  2. Filter Integrity: In a Filter, you MUST return the modified data. Failure to do so will result in null being passed to the next hook, potentially breaking the system.
  3. Global Scoping: If using class methods as hooks, use the array syntax: Hooks::attach('hook', [ $this, 'myMethod' ]);.

[!TIP] Extensibility: When building a module, consider creating your own custom hooks using Hooks::run() and Hooks::filter(). This allows other developers to extend your module's functionality without modifying your core files.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• System Class — How the bootstrap process triggers the init hook.
  • Theme Class — How themes utilize hooks for asset loading.
  • Using Hooks Guide — Comprehensive guide on creating event-driven extensions.

docs.title: HTTP Class
docs.text: The Http class provides utilities for network requests, URL validation, security header management (via System), and client interaction (redirection).

docs.title: validateUrl()
docs.text: Checks if a URL is valid, safe, and adheres to port/protocol constraints. Http::validateUrl(string $url);

docs.title: isLocal()
docs.text: Determines if an IP or URL points to a local network address (e.g., 127.0.0.1, 192.168.x.x). Http::isLocal(string $url);

docs.title: validateProtocol()
docs.text: Ensures the URL uses an allowed protocol (e.g., ['http', 'https']). Http::validateProtocol(string $url, array $allowed_protocols);

docs.title: fetch()
docs.text:
Retrieves content from a remote URL. Supports both file_get_contents and cURL. 

// Simple GET
$html = Http::fetch("https://example.com");

// Advanced cURL request
$data = Http::fetch([
    'url'  => 'https://api.example.com',
    'curl' => true,
    'curl_options' => [
        CURLOPT_POST => 1,
        CURLOPT_POSTFIELDS => ['key' => 'val']
    ]
]);

docs.title: ipDetail()
docs.text: Fetches geographical information for a given IP address. $info = Http::ipDetail(string $ip);

docs.title: getIpCountry()
docs.text: Returns the two-letter country code for an IP. $country = Http::getIpCountry(string $ip);

docs.title: randAgent()
docs.text: Returns a random, valid browser User-Agent string from the system's registry.

docs.title: getBearerToken()
docs.text: Extracts the Bearer token from the Authorization header. $token = Http::getBearerToken();

docs.title: getAuthorizationHeader()
docs.text: Retrieves the raw Authorization header, accounting for various server environments (Nginx, Apache, CGI).

docs.title: redirect()
docs.text: Safely redirects the user to a new URL. Uses header("Location: ...") if headers haven't been sent, otherwise falls back to a Javascript/Meta-refresh redirect. Http::redirect(string $url);

docs.title: Image Processing & Asset Optimization Class
docs.text: The Image class is the primary visual asset engine for GeniXCMS. It provides high-performance image manipulation, on-the-fly thumbnail generation with intelligent caching, multi-format conversion (WebP), and automated watermark injection powered by the Intervention Image v3 library and native GD/Imagick drivers.

docs.title: ⚡ Integrated Thumbnail Engine
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a "just-in-time" thumbnail system. Images are processed only upon first request and then served as static files from the assets/cache/thumbs/ directory for sub-second performance.

docs.title: Image::thumbFly($img, $type, $size, $align)
docs.text:
The modern, recommended thumbnail generator using Intervention Image.

Parameter Type Description $img string\|array Source image (URL or local path). $type string Type of thumbnail: 'square', 'large', 'small', or 'crop'. $size int\|string Dimension value (e.g., 150 or '300x200'). $align string Alignment configuration for cropping.
  • Caching: Automatically saves processed images to assets/cache/thumbs/.
  • Watermarking: Injects brand overlays if media_use_watermark is enabled and the image width exceeds 200px.
  • Remote Support: Automatically fetches and processes remote images (e.g., from CDNs).

docs.title: Image::convertWebp(string $img)
docs.text:
Converts a local image asset into the WebP format using Intervention Image.

  • Quality: Compresses at 65% quality for optimal balance.
  • Output: Returns the new WebP file path or false on failure.

docs.title: Image::compressPng(string $img)
docs.text: Compresses a PNG image using GD's high compression (quality 9) while maintaining alpha transparency.

docs.title: Image::compressPng2(string $path, int $max_quality = 80)
docs.text: Advanced PNG compression using the pngquant shell utility (if available on the server).

docs.title: Image::compressJpg(string $path, int $quality = 80)
docs.text: Compresses a JPEG image using the Imagick extension for superior results.

docs.title: Image::resize(string $src, string $dst, int $width, int $height, bool $crop = false)
docs.text:
A native GD-based utility for creating explicit file copies with new dimensions. Supports BMP, GIF, JPG, and PNG.

// Center-crop a hero image to exactly 1200x400
Image::resize($original, $banner, 1200, 400, true);

docs.title: Image::isJpg(string &$pict) / Image::isPng(string &$pict)
docs.text: Quick utility methods to check file extensions.

docs.title: Image::getGravatar(string $email, int $s = 60, ...)
docs.text:
Retrieves a Gravatar URL or a full <img> tag for a given email address.

Parameter Type Default Description $email string Required The user's email address. $s int 60 Size in pixels. $d string 'mm' Default image set (mystery man). $r string 'g' Maximum rating. $img bool false If true, returns a full <img /> tag. $atts array [] Optional HTML attributes for the tag.
  • Offline Support: Automatically falls back to a local default avatar if the site is in offline mode.

docs.title: 🏹 Smart URL Pattern (Dynamic Assets)
docs.text:
Thumbnails can be retrieved dynamically via human-readable URL paths resolved by the Router.

  • Square Icon: /thumb/type/square/size/80/uploads/my-photo.jpg
  • Large Preview: /thumb/type/large/size/800/uploads/banner.png
  • Custom Crop: /thumb/type/crop/size/400x300/uploads/hero.jpg

docs.title: 🛠️ Security & Integrity
docs.text:
• Transparency: Both resize and compressPng fully preserve alpha channels for PNG and GIF files.
  • Watermark Engine: If media_use_watermark is enabled, images larger than 200px automatically receive the configured brand overlay at the specified position and opacity.

[!IMPORTANT] Server Requirements: This class requires the PHP GD Extension (with WebP support). For advanced features, Imagick and pngquant are recommended.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Upload Class — Handling the initial ingestion of media.
  • Router Class — How the /thumb/ URL pattern is resolved.
  • Media Settings — Configuring watermarks and thumbnail defaults.

docs.title: Install Class
docs.text: The Install class manages the initial setup of the GeniXCMS environment, including configuration file generation and database schema installation for multiple drivers (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite).

docs.title: makeConfig()
docs.text: Generates the inc/config/config.php file based on user input (via Session). - Generates a unique 200-character SECURITY_KEY. - Generates a unique 20-character SITE_ID. - Configures database credentials and driver constants. $result = Install::makeConfig(string $file_path);

docs.title: createTable()
docs.text:
Creates the necessary SQL schema for all core system tables. - MySQL: InnoDB with utf8 charset. - PostgreSQL: Uses serial/bigserial types. - SQLite: Compatible with local file-based storage.

Tables created: cat, cat_param, menus, options, posts, posts_param, user, user_detail, comments, widgets, permissions.

docs.title: insertData()
docs.text: Populates the newly created database with: 1. Initial Options: Sets up site metadata, system versions, default themes (default), and active modules (gxeditor). 2. Default Content: Creates default categories (News, Tutorials) and initial posts/pages (Welcome, About Us, Privacy Policy). 3. Menu Structure: Sets up the primary and footer menu locations. Install::insertData();

docs.title: Language & Localization Class
docs.text: The Language class is the core engine behind GeniXCMS's global reach. It manages Internationalization (i18n) and Localization (l10n) by handling locale detection, user preferences, and the dynamic rendering of multi-language content across the system.

docs.title: Language::isActive()
docs.text:
Detects the currently active 2-letter ISO language code (e.g., 'en', 'id', 'es').

  • Logic: It prioritizes the URL locale parameter first, falling back to the session value, and finally the system default.
  • Return: string

docs.title: Language::setActive(string $lang_code)
docs.text: Programmatically forces the active language for the current browser session. Typically used when a user manually clicks a language flag.

docs.title: 🌎 Content Translation
docs.text: GeniXCMS supports per-post translation by storing localized metadata in the postparam table.

docs.title: Language::getLangParam(string $lang, int $post_id)
docs.text:
Retrieves translated block (Title or Body) for a specific post.

// Retrieve the Indonesian title for post ID 42
$translated_title = Language::getLangParam('id', 42);

docs.title: Language::flagList()
docs.text:
Generates a semantic HTML list (<ul>) of language switching flags.

  • Automation: It automatically injects the necessary flag-icon-css library from its CDN into the page footer.
  • Config: It pulls available languages from the multilang_country system option.
// In your theme's header.php
<?= Language::flagList(); ?>

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration Schema
docs.text:
The behavior of the Language engine is dictated by several global options found in the Settings Dashboard.

Option Key Description multilang_enable Global toggle for translation features. multilang_default The primary ISO code for the website (e.g., en). multilang_country A JSON manifest mapping codes to display names and flag icons.

docs.title: 🛠️ Translation Best Practices
docs.text:
• Shorthand: Use the global _('Translation Key') function (aliased to Gettext) for system and theme messages.
  • Post Translations: When creating content, ensure you use the Translate tab in the Post Editor to define version for each active locale.
  • Assets: Theme-specific translations should be placed in inc/themes/{your-theme}/inc/lang/.

[!TIP] SEO Localization: The Language class integrates with the Router to support path-based locale detection (e.g., /en/post.html). Always ensure your theme's <html> tag uses lang="{$website_lang}" for correct search engine indexing.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Multi-Language User Guide — Configuring languages in the admin panel.
  • Router Class — How the system detects language codes in the URL.

docs.title: Mail Transmission Class
docs.text: The Mail class is a high-level wrapper around the PHPMailer library. It simplifies the process of sending complex, multi-part emails (HTML and Plain Text) by abstracting the underlying transport mechanism — allowing your application to switch seamlessly between standard PHP mail() and authenticated SMTP relay.

docs.title: Mail::send(array $vars)
docs.text:
This is the primary way to dispatch communications from your modules or themes.

Parameter Type Required Description to string Yes The recipient's email address. to_name string No Display name for the recipient. subject string Yes The header subject line of the email. message string Yes The body content (HTML or Plain Text). msgtype string No Content format: 'html' (default) or 'text'.

Returns: true on success, or a string containing the detailed error message from the mailer engine on failure.

$vars = [
    'to'      => 'clark@example.com',
    'to_name' => 'Clark Kent',
    'subject' => 'System Notification',
    'message' => '<h1>Update!</h1><p>Your content has been published.</p>',
    'msgtype' => 'html'
];

$result = Mail::send($vars);
if ($result !== true) {
    Log::error("Mail failed: " . $result);
}

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration Schema
docs.text:
The behavior of the Mail engine is governed by the settings defined in Settings > E-Mail.

  • mailtype: Toggle between 0 (Local PHP Mail) and 1 (External SMTP Relay).
  • smtphost: The address of your SMTP provider (e.g., smtp.gmail.com).
  • smtpport: The connection port (usually 465 for SSL or 587 for TLS).
  • smtpuser: Your authentication username/email.
  • smtppass: Your authentication password.

docs.title: 🏗️ Internal Workflow
docs.text:
The Mail class performs several automated steps during transmission:

  1. Configuration Fetch: Retrieves all SMTP and Site parameters from the Options table.
  2. Engine Boot: Instantiates the PHPMailer object with the appropriate encryption (SSL/TLS).
  3. Sender Identification: Automatically uses the site's default name and email as the "From" identity.
  4. Error Handling: Wraps the transmission in an exception block, returning the raw error if the server rejects the request.

[!IMPORTANT] SMTP Delivery: For high-volume or critical transactional emails (like password resets), it is strongly recommended to use a dedicated SMTP service (SendGrid, Mailgun, or Gmail) rather than the local PHP mail() function to avoid being flagged as spam.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• E-Mail User Guide — Configuring mail delivery in the dashboard.
  • Vendor Class — How PHPMailer is integrated into the system.

docs.title: Marketplace Class Interface
docs.text: The Marketplace class provides an interface to the official GeniXCMS Marketplace API. It allows the system to search for and install themes and modules directly from the centralized repository.

docs.title: search()
docs.text:
Queries the GeniXCMS marketplace for available items.

Syntax: 

public static function search(string $query = '', string $type = 'theme', int $page = 1) : array

Parameters:

  • $query (string): The search keyword. If empty, returns latest items.
  • $type (string): The item type (theme or module). Default is theme.
  • $page (int): Navigation page number. Default is 1.

Returns:

  • array: Evaluation result containing items data or error status.

Example Usage in a Module: 

$results = Marketplace::search('responsive', 'theme');
if ($results['status'] === 'success') {
    foreach ($results['data'] as $item) {
        echo $item['name'];
    }
}

docs.title: install()
docs.text:
Downloads and installs a package from the marketplace. It handles the full lifecycle from fetching item details to ZIP extraction and security scanning.

Syntax: 

public static function install(string $id, string $type, string $license = '', string $domain = '') : array

Workflow:

  1. Detail Retrieval: Fetches the official download URL from the marketplace API.
  2. Download: Uses CURL to download the ZIP package to inc/cache/.
  3. Security Scan: Runs Security::scanZip() on the temporary file before extraction.
  4. Extraction: Unzips the package into the appropriate directory (GX_THEME or GX_MOD).
  5. Post-Install Hooks: Triggers theme_install_action or module_install_action upon completion.

Parameters:

  • $id (string): Unique marketplace item identifier.
  • $type (string): Item type (theme or module).
  • $license (string): Optional license key for premium items.
  • $domain (string): Optional domain for license validation.

Returns:

  • array: An array with status (bool), message (string), and optional errors (array) if security checks fail.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Integrity
docs.text:
The Marketplace class integrates deeply with the GeniXCMS security infrastructure:

  • Pre-Extraction Scanning: No files are written to the live theme or module directories until the ZIP has been scanned for malicious code or prohibited file types.
  • User Agent Validation: All API requests identify the client as GeniXCMS/{version} to ensure proper repository logging.
  • SSL Verification: Although verification is currently bypassed in the code (CURLOPT_SSL_VERIFYPEER => false) for maximum compatibility with varied server environments, it is recommended to ensure your server's CA bundles are up to date.

docs.title: ⚓ Installation Hooks
docs.text:
Modules can listen to these hooks to perform additional setup (like database migrations) after a marketplace installation:

Hook Type Description theme_install_action Action Fired after a theme is successfully extracted. module_install_action Action Fired after a module is successfully extracted.

docs.title: $apiUrl
docs.text:
The base endpoint for the official marketplace service.

  • Value: https://genixcms.web.id/api/v1/marketplace/

docs.title: MarketplaceAjax Class
docs.text: The MarketplaceAjax class provides a set of AJAX endpoints for interacting with the GeniXCMS official marketplace. It allows users to search for themes and modules and install them directly from the dashboard.

docs.title: index($param = null)
docs.text:
The main entry point for MarketplaceAjax. It dispatches the request to a specific action based on the action parameter in the URL.

  • Endpoint: index.php?ajax=marketplace&action=ACTION_NAME
  • Actions: search, install.

docs.title: search($param = null)
docs.text:
Searches the official marketplace for themes or modules.

  • URL Parameters:
    • q (string): Search keyword.
    • type (string): Item type (theme or module).
    • page (int): Page number (default: 1).
  • Response: Returns a JSON object with a list of matching items.

docs.title: install($param = null)
docs.text:
Initiates the download and installation of a marketplace item.

  • URL Parameters:
    • id (int): Unique marketplace item ID.
    • type (string): Item type (theme or module).
    • license_key (string): Optional license key for premium items.
    • domain (string): Optional domain for license validation.
  • Workflow: Calls Marketplace::install() internally, which handles the download, security scanning, and extraction.
  • Response: Returns a JSON object with the installation status and message.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Authentication
docs.text:
• Authentication: All actions require a valid system token and the user must have at least level 1 (Admin) access.
  • XSS Protection: All query parameters are sanitized using Typo::int() and Typo::cleanX().
  • Marketplace API Integration: Securely communicates with the GeniXCMS marketplace API at https://genixcms.web.id/api/v1/marketplace/.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ajax Class — The main AJAX dispatcher.
  • Marketplace Class — The core marketplace engine.
  • Security Class — Automated ZIP scanning during installation.
  • Token Class — Handling security tokens.

docs.title: Navigation Menu Class
docs.text: The Menus class is the primary engine for constructing, managing, and rendering site-wide navigation systems in GeniXCMS. It supports multi-level nested hierarchies (up to 4 levels) and features intelligent link resolution for static pages, categories, and module-based URLs.

docs.title: Menus::getMenu(string $menuid, ...)
docs.text:
The primary method for injecting a navigation structure into your theme's templates. Supports up to 4 levels of nested menus with customizable styling per theme.

Parameter Type Default Description $menuid string Required The unique identifier matching the database (e.g., mainmenu). $class string '' Custom CSS class appended to the root <ul> element. $bsnav bool false If true, it generates Bootstrap 5 compatible dropdown markup with nav-item, nav-link, dropdown-menu, etc. $itemClass string '' NEW Theme-specific CSS classes applied to every menu <a> tag. Allows custom styling without compromising Bootstrap structure.

Returns: string (HTML fragment).

docs.title: Basic Examples
docs.text:
{* Simple menu *}
{Menus::getMenu('top-menu')|noescape}

{* With container classes *}
{Menus::getMenu('top-menu', 'navbar-nav ms-auto', true)|noescape}

{* With theme-specific item styling (NEW) *}
{Menus::getMenu('top-menu', 'navbar-nav ms-auto', true, 'px-4 py-2 hover:text-blue-500 transition-colors')|noescape}

{* TailwindCSS-styled horizontal menu *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'flex flex-row gap-8 items-center', true, 'block px-3 py-2 rounded hover:bg-gray-100')|noescape}

{* Vertical sidebar menu *}
{Menus::getMenu('sidebar', 'flex flex-col space-y-2', false, 'w-full px-4 py-2 text-left hover:bg-blue-50 rounded')|noescape}

docs.title: The itemClass Parameter (NEW in v2.0.1)
docs.text:
The itemClass parameter lets themes add custom styling to menu links while maintaining Bootstrap dropdown compatibility:

{* Before: Bootstrap-only styling *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'navbar-nav', true)|noescape}
<!-- Links get nav-link, dropdown-item classes -->

{* After: Bootstrap + Theme-specific styling *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'flex flex-row gap-8', true, 'block px-4 py-2 hover:text-primary transition-colors')|noescape}
<!-- Links get BOTH Bootstrap classes AND custom itemClass -->

Sample HTML Output: 

<ul class="menu-mainmenu flex flex-row gap-8">
  <li class="nav-item">
    <a href="/about" class="nav-link block px-4 py-2 hover:text-primary transition-colors">
      About
    </a>
  </li>
  <li class="nav-item dropdown">
    <a href="javascript:void(0)" class="nav-link dropdown-toggle block px-4 py-2 hover:text-primary transition-colors" 
       data-bs-toggle="dropdown" aria-expanded="false">
      Services
    </a>
    <ul class="dropdown-menu">
      <li>
        <a href="/services/web" class="dropdown-item block px-4 py-2 hover:text-primary transition-colors">
          Web Design
        </a>
      </li>
    </ul>
  </li>
</ul>

docs.title: Bootstrap Mode: $bsnav = true
docs.text:
When Bootstrap mode is enabled, the menu automatically applies Bootstrap 5 navigation classes:

{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'navbar-nav', true)|noescape}

Classes Applied Automatically: - Root level with children: class="nav-item dropdown" - Links with dropdowns: class="nav-link dropdown-toggle" data-bs-toggle="dropdown" - Submenus: class="dropdown-menu" - Submenu items: class="dropdown-item"

Use Bootstrap mode when: - You're using Bootstrap for styling - You want automatic dropdown functionality - Supporting existing Bootstrap-dependent themes

docs.title: Custom Item Styling: Use $itemClass
docs.text:
When you want custom styling without Bootstrap constraints, use the new itemClass parameter:

{* Material Design inspired menu *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'flex flex-row gap-4', true, 
  'px-4 py-2 rounded-lg font-medium hover:bg-blue-100 hover:text-blue-700 transition-all')|noescape}

What itemClass does: - Applied to every <a> tag in the menu (all 4 depth levels) - Works alongside Bootstrap classes when $bsnav = true - Pure CSS customization—no conflict with existing markup - Enables TailwindCSS, custom CSS, or any framework

docs.title: Example 1: Modern Horizontal Menu with Tailwind
docs.text:
<nav class="bg-white shadow-md">
  {Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 
    'flex flex-row gap-0 items-center', 
    true, 
    'block px-4 py-3 border-b-4 border-transparent hover:border-blue-500 font-medium text-gray-700 hover:text-blue-600 transition-colors duration-200')|noescape}
</nav>

docs.title: Example 2: Vertical Sidebar (Non-Bootstrap)
docs.text:
<aside class="bg-gray-50 border-r border-gray-200 p-4">
  {Menus::getMenu('sidebar', 
    'flex flex-col space-y-2', 
    false, 
    'w-full block px-4 py-3 text-left rounded-lg border-l-4 border-transparent hover:border-blue-500 hover:bg-blue-50 transition-all')|noescape}
</aside>

docs.title: Example 3: Mobile-Responsive Menu
docs.text:
{* Responsive menu that adapts to screen size *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 
  'flex flex-col md:flex-row md:gap-8 gap-2', 
  true, 
  'block w-full md:w-auto px-4 py-2 md:py-3 rounded md:rounded-none hover:bg-blue-50 md:hover:bg-transparent md:hover:border-b-2 md:hover:border-blue-500')|noescape}

docs.title: Example 4: Material Design Icons Integration
docs.text:
{* Menu with icon support via custom classes *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 
  'flex flex-row gap-6', 
  true, 
  'flex items-center gap-2 px-3 py-2 rounded-full hover:bg-blue-100 hover:shadow-md')|noescape}

{* Icons would be added via custom menu item classes in database *}

docs.title: 🔄 Menu Depth Levels
docs.text:
The menu system supports up to 4 depth levels, each with specific HTML structure:

Level Bootstrap Class Custom Usage Typical Use Case 1 (Root) nav-item + dropdown (if has children) Top-level item styling Primary navigation 2 (Submenu) dropdown-item First-level dropdown styling Service categories 3 (Sub-submenu) dropdown-item Nested dropdown styling Subcategories 4 (Deep) dropdown-item Deepest level styling Rare—use sparingly for UX

Nesting Recommendation: Keep navigation to 2–3 levels for optimal UX and accessibility.

The class also provides several utilities for retrieving and manipulating menu items programmatically.

  • Menus::getMenuRaw(string $menuid): Returns an unformatted array of menu objects for the specified ID.
  • Menus::id(int $id): Retrieves a specific menu item object by its primary key.
  • Menus::getMenuAdmin(string $menuid): Generates the interactive, drag-and-drop management interface used in the administration dashboard.

docs.title: 🛠️ CRUD Operations
docs.text:
These methods are typically used within the administrative controllers to handle the navigation lifecycle.

Method Description Menus::insert(array $vars) Dispatches a new menu item to the database. Menus::update(array $vars) Modifies existing item parameters like labels, parents, or URLs. Menus::delete(int $id) Permanently removes an item and its children. Menus::updateMenuOrder(array $vars) Updates the vertical stacking order based on drag-and-drop feedback.

docs.title: 🔗 Intelligent Link Resolution
docs.text:
The Menus class integrates deeply with the Url Class, allowing it to automatically resolve the correct destination based on the item type (e.g., Page, Post, Category, or Mod). This ensures that your navigation remains functional even if you switch between Standard and Smart URL modes.

[!TIP] NEW in v2.0.1: The itemClass parameter enables theme-specific styling without breaking Bootstrap compatibility. All themes can now apply custom styling while maintaining support for dependent modules and plugins.

Backward Compatible: Existing code using getMenu('menuid') or getMenu('menuid', '', true) continues to work unchanged.

docs.title: 🔄 Backward Compatibility & Migration
docs.text:
The new itemClass parameter is fully backward compatible:

Old Code New Code Behavior Menus::getMenu('mainmenu') ✅ Still works No itemClass applied Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'navbar-nav', true) ✅ Still works Bootstrap styling, no custom item classes Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', '', true, 'custom-class') ✨ NEW Bootstrap styling + custom item classes

No breaking changes—all existing themes and code continue to function unchanged.

docs.title: Upgrading Your Theme
docs.text:
If your theme currently uses plain Bootstrap menus and you want to add custom styling:

{* Before (Bootstrap only) *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'navbar-nav', true)|noescape}

{* After (Bootstrap + Custom styling) *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 'navbar-nav', true, 'hover:text-blue-500 transition-colors')|noescape}

docs.title: 💡 Next Steps for Theme Developers
docs.text:
1. Review your theme template(s) where getMenu() is called
  2. Define custom itemClass values that match your theme's CSS framework
  3. Test dropdown behavior to ensure Bootstrap interactions still work (if using $bsnav = true)
  4. Document theme-specific menu styling for other developers

docs.title: Example: Creating a Custom Theme with Menu Styling
docs.text:
<?php
// in inc/themes/mytheme/function.php

class MyTheme {
  public static function getMenuClasses($variant = 'default') {
    $cssClasses = [
      'default' => 'block px-4 py-2 hover:text-blue-500 transition-colors',
      'subtle' => 'px-3 py-2 text-gray-600 hover:text-gray-900 font-medium',
      'bold' => 'px-4 py-3 font-bold text-lg hover:bg-blue-100 rounded-lg',
    ];

    return $cssClasses[$variant] ?? $cssClasses['default'];
  }
}
?>

{* in inc/themes/mytheme/header.php *}
{Menus::getMenu('mainmenu', 
  'flex flex-row gap-8 navbar', 
  true, 
  MyTheme::getMenuClasses('bold'))|noescape}

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Menus User Guide — Visually building navigations in the dashboard.
  • Url Class — How the system generates destination links for menu items.

docs.title: Migration Builder Class
docs.text: The Migration class is the foundation of GeniXCMS's database versioning system. It allows developers to define schema changes (tables, columns, indexes) in PHP code, ensuring consistent database states across all development and production environments.

docs.title: ⚙️ How it Works
docs.text:
Each migration is a standalone PHP file containing a class that implements two critical directions:

  • up(): Defines the changes to be applied (e.g., CREATE TABLE).
  • down(): Defines how to reverse those changes (e.g., DROP TABLE).

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating a Migration
docs.text: Migrations are stored in inc/migrations/ and must follow a strict naming and class structure to be discovered by the engine.

docs.title: 📂 File Naming Convention
docs.text:
The filename must follow the pattern: YYYYMMDD-batchid-Description.php

  • Example: 20240327-01-CreatePermissionsTable.php

docs.title: 📂 Class Naming Convention
docs.text:
The class name must match the filename, replacing dashes with underscores and prefixing with Migration_.

  • Example: class Migration_20240327_01_CreatePermissionsTable extends Migration

docs.title: 🚀 Execution via CLI
docs.text:
The genix console provides built-in commands to manage your migration stack.

Command Description php genix migrate Scans for pending files and executes their up() methods in chronological order. php genix migrate:rollback Identifies the last execution batch and runs their down() methods to undo changes.

docs.title: 📝 Example Implementation
docs.text:
<?php

class Migration_20240327_10_AddProductTable extends Migration
{
    /**
     * Apply schema changes.
     */
    public function up()
    {
        $sql = "CREATE TABLE `products` (
            `id` int(11) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
            `name` varchar(255) NOT NULL,
            `price` decimal(10,2) NOT NULL
        ) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8;";

        Db::query($sql);
    }

    /**
     * Revert schema changes.
     */
    public function down()
    {
        Db::query("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `products` ");
    }
}

docs.title: ⚡ Method Reference
docs.text:
• Migration::init(): System internal. Ensures the migrations tracking table exists.
  • Migration::run(): Dispatches the migration execution logic.
  • Migration::rollback(): Dispatches the rollback logic.

[!IMPORTANT] Data Safety: Always double-check your down() method. A well-written rollback is just as important as the migration itself to prevent manual database cleanup during failed deployments.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• CLI Console — Full list of genix commands.
  • Db Class — Understanding how to execute raw SQL within migrations.

docs.title: Mod Class
docs.text: The Mod class is the heart of the GeniXCMS modular architecture. It manages the discovery, metadata parsing, activation, and loading of all system modules.

docs.title: Module Metadata
docs.text:
Modules are defined by a standard DocBlock in their index.php file. 

/**
 * Name: My Awesome Module
 * Desc: Description of the module
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Developer: Your Name
 * Icon: bi bi-cpu
 */

docs.title: data()
docs.text: Parses the module's index.php and returns an array of metadata. $meta = Mod::data(string $mod_name);

docs.title: load()
docs.text: Safely includes the module's index.php file once. This prevents class redeclaration errors when modules are re-activated or re-loaded. Mod::load('my_module');

docs.title: activate()
docs.text: Registers a module in the system and runs its mod_activate and {mod}_activate hooks (ideal for table creation). Mod::activate('ecommerce_mod');

docs.title: deactivate()
docs.text: Removes a module from the active list and runs its mod_deactivate and {mod}_deactivate hooks. Mod::deactivate('ecommerce_mod');

docs.title: Deletion (handled by Mod::loader)
docs.text: When a module is removed via the admin UI, GeniXCMS executes the mod_delete and {mod}_delete hooks before removing files from disk. This allows for database cleanup (e.g., dropping tables).

docs.title: isActive()
docs.text: Checks if a specific module folder is enabled in the database. if (Mod::isActive('seonix')) { ... }

docs.title: inc()
docs.text: Includes a module partial (PHP/HTML) and extracts data into it. Returns the output as a string. echo Mod::inc('list_view', ['items' => $data], GX_MOD.'my_mod/');

docs.title: url()
docs.text: Generates the public base URL for a module (e.g., for assets). echo Mod::url('my_mod'); // http://site.com/inc/mod/my_mod

docs.title: modList()
docs.text: Scans the inc/mod/ directory and returns all valid modules.

docs.title: addMenuList()
docs.text: Registers module keys into the global module registry. Mod::addMenuList(['my_mod' => 'Module Display Name']);

docs.title: Model Class (ORM / ActiveRecord)
docs.text: The Model class provides a robust, ActiveRecord-style ORM for interacting with your database. It allows you to treat database rows as native PHP objects, inheriting complex data manipulation capabilities like finding, saving, updating, and deleting with minimal boilerplate code.

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating Your Model
docs.text:
To create a domain entity, simply extend the Model class and define your target table name.

/**
 * Example: Custom Book Model
 */
class Books extends Model 
{
    protected $table = 'books';
    protected $primaryKey = 'id'; // Default is 'id'
}

docs.title: 🔍 Finding & Retrieving Records
docs.text: The Model class features a fluent interface for data retrieval.

docs.title: Model::find(int $id)
docs.text:
Quickly retrieve a single record by its primary key. Returns a model instance or null.

$book = Books::find(12);
if ($book) {
    echo $book->title;
}

docs.title: Model::all()
docs.text: Returns a collection (array of objects) containing all records from the table.

docs.title: 💾 Saving & Updating Data
docs.text: The save() method intelligently determines whether to execute an INSERT or an UPDATE based on the existence of the primary key.

docs.title: Creating New Records
docs.text:
// Instantiate and set properties
$book = new Books();
$book->title = "The Art of CMS";
$book->author = "GeniXCMS Team";
$book->save(); // Performs INSERT

// Or use the static shorthand
Books::create([
    'title' => 'Fast Lane',
    'status' => '1'
]);

docs.title: Updating Existing Records
docs.text:
$book = Books::find(12);
$book->title = "Updated Title";
$book->save(); // Performs UPDATE

docs.title: ⚡ Chainable Query Integration
docs.text:
Models integrate natively with the Query Builder, allowing you to start complex queries directly from your class.

// Find specific records using chaining
$active_books = Books::where('status', '1')
    ->orderBy('date', 'desc')
    ->limit(10)
    ->get();

docs.title: 📝 Technical Method Summary
docs.text: Method Role Return Type all() Retrieve all table records. array<objects> find($id) Look up a single record by its PK. Model \| null where() Initiate a fluent query condition. QueryBuilder create($data) Static shorthand for immediate insertion. bool save() Persistent save (Insert/Update) of the current object. bool delete() Permanent removal of the current object. bool

docs.title: 🔮 Dynamic Attributes
docs.text:
GeniXCMS uses PHP Magic Methods (__get and __set) to map database columns to object properties. This ensures your code remains clean and intuitive.

$post = Posts::find(42);
echo $post->content; // Automatically resolves to the 'content' column

[!IMPORTANT] Data Integrity: When using Model::save(), ensure your table's primary key is correctly defined in the $primaryKey property if it deviates from the standard id. Failure to do so may result in duplicate records.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Query Builder — Mastering complex SQL queries through the model layer.
  • Db Class — Direct database interaction for non-model logic.

docs.title: Global Options & Settings Class
docs.text: The Options class is the central repository for site-wide configurations in GeniXCMS. It manages the persistent storage and efficient retrieval of everything from core metadata (Site Name, E-mail) to granular plugin parameters and theme-specific settings.

docs.title: ⚡ Data Retrieval Patterns
docs.text: Fetching configuration data must be efficient to ensure sub-second page loads. GeniXCMS provides two distinct retrieval pathways.

docs.title: Options::v(string $key)
docs.text:
This is the High-Performance method. It retrieves values from the system's pre-loaded data object (internal cache), which is initialized during the core boot phase.

  • Best Use Case: Use this in themes and templates to avoid redundant database queries.
  • Example: <?= Options::v('site_name'); ?>

docs.title: Options::get(string $key, bool $decode = true)
docs.text:
This method performs a Direct Database Query for the specified key.

  • Best Use Case: Use this when you need to ensure you have the most up-to-the-second value, or when fetching large JSON objects from modules.
  • Example: $raw_json = Options::get('my_module_config');

docs.title: 💾 Saving & Modifying Data
docs.text: The Options engine supports both single-key updates and batch processing.

docs.title: Options::update($input, $value = '')
docs.text:
The primary method for editing site configuration.

Scenario Input Type Usage Example Single Key string Options::update('site_email', 'new@site.com'); Batch Update array Options::update(['A' => '1', 'B' => '2']);

docs.title: Options::insert(array $vars)
docs.text: Dispatches multiple new options to the database simultaneously. Typically used during module installation or theme activation.

docs.title: 🔍 Validation & Existence
docs.text:
Before attempting to use or update a setting, you can verify its existence.

  • Options::isExist(string $key): Returns a bool indicating if the key is present in the database.
  • Options::validate(string $key): Checks if the key exists and has a non-empty value.

docs.title: 🏗️ Technical Method Summary
docs.text:
Method Role Return Type v($key) Fetch from pre-loaded internal cache. mixed get($key) Direct database retrieval. mixed update() Modify existing option(s). bool insert() Add multiple new options. bool isExist() Check for key presence. bool

[!TIP] Performance Hint: Always prioritize Options::v() within your theme's header.php or footer.php. This method allows GeniXCMS to serve the entire site configuration with zero additional SQL overhead after the initial boot.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• System Configuration Guide — Managing these values via the dashboard.
  • OptionsBuilder Guide — Creating automated settings panels for themes.

docs.title: OptionsBuilder Class API Reference
docs.text: The OptionsBuilder class is a premium UI orchestration engine specifically designed for theme and module settings panels. It generates an industrial, ERP-style dashboard with integrated dynamic CSS generation for the frontend.

docs.title: new OptionsBuilder(array $opt, array $presets = [], ...)
docs.text:
Initializes the builder with saved data and UI configuration.

  • $opt: The currently saved options (key-value pairs from the database).
  • $presets: (Optional) Array of color scheme presets for one-click application.
  • $panel_palettes: (Optional) Specialized color palettes for custom panel blocks.
  • $config: Identity configuration:
    • brandName: The name of your theme/module.
    • brandVer: The version string.
    • brandAbbr: 2-3 character abbreviation for the logo.
    • brandIcon: FontAwesome icon class (overrides abbreviation).
    • brandColor: Primary brand HEX color (used for gradients).
    • saveKey: The name attribute of the submit button.

docs.title: render(array $schema): void
docs.text:
Generates the full administrative interface within a POST form.

  • How it works:
    1. Injects required CSS and JS globally.
    2. Renders the sticky Topbar with branding and the Save button.
    3. Renders the Sidebar with grouped tab navigation.
    4. Renders the Main Content panels containing cards and fields.
    5. Wraps everything in a secure form with a CSRF token.

docs.title: static generateFrontendCSS(array $opt, array $config = []): string
docs.text:
Static utility to generate minified CSS and Google Fonts assets for the website frontend.

  • Parameters:
    • $opt: The options array containing saved values.
    • $config: Configuration for the CSS engine:
      • themeUrl: Absolute path to your theme folder.
      • minify: Boolean to enable CSS minification.
      • loadStyleCss: Whether to auto-include the theme's style.css.
      • extraCssRules: Custom CSS strings to append.

docs.title: static colorField(string $name, string $val, string $id = ''): string
docs.text: A standalone utility to render the GeniXCMS standard color picker.

docs.title: 📋 Schema Architecture
docs.text:
OptionsBuilder uses a 3-tier hierarchy: Tabs → Cards → Fields.

Level Key Description Tab id, label, icon, group Defines the sidebar navigation item. Card title, cols, fields A grouped container of settings. cols supports 1, 2, or 3. Field type, name, label, hint The actual input element (text, color, select, etc.).

docs.title: 🛠️ Supported Field Types
docs.text:
The following input types are supported within the fields array of a card:

Type Description Key Properties text Standard single-line text input. name, label, placeholder, hint password Secure password input field. name, label, placeholder textarea Multi-line text area with auto-expansion. name, label, rows, hint color Premium color picker with HEX text synchronization. name, label, id number Numeric input with validation. name, label, min, max, step range Range slider with a dynamic value badge. name, label, min, max, step, unit select Standard dropdown selection menu. name, label, options (assoc array) toggle Segmented On/Off switch (returns on/off). name, label, yes (label), no (label) checkbox Single checkbox switch (returns on/off). name, label typo_row Composite typography group (Family, Size, Weight, Color). label, prefix heading Small uppercase section heading within a card. text divider Subtle horizontal separator line. - raw Direct raw HTML injection. html

docs.title: 📂 Tab & Panel Types
docs.text:
OptionsBuilder supports different specialized panel layouts via the type property in the tab definition:

Tab Type Description standard (Default) Uses sections and cards to organize settings. presets Displays a grid of color scheme presets defined in the constructor. panels A dynamic block builder for mapping categories to accent colors/fonts.

docs.title: 🎨 Card Configuration
docs.text:
Cards are the primary containers for fields. They support grid layouts:

Property Type Description title string The header text for the card. cols int Number of columns (1, 2, or 3). Default: 1. fields array List of field definitions to render inside the card. type string Set to typo_row or raw for specialized card behavior.

docs.title: typo_row
docs.text: A powerful composite element that combines font family, size, weight, and color into a single row. When used with standard prefixes (e.g., typo_body), the Dynamic CSS Engine automatically generates the corresponding frontend styles.

docs.title: presets
docs.text: The presets tab type allows users to quickly apply a group of settings (usually colors) with one click. These are passed to the OptionsBuilder constructor.

docs.title: panels (Panel Builder)
docs.text: A specialized tab type that allows users to configure up to 5 content blocks, mapping categories to specific accent colors, background images, and typography.

docs.title: ⚡ Reactivity & CSS Generation
docs.text:
OptionsBuilder is not just a UI; it's a CSS Engine. By using standardized prefixes in your field names, the generateFrontendCSS() method can automatically map these to CSS variables and selectors:

  • Colors: link_color, body_background_color, etc.
  • Typography: Any name ending in _font, _size, _weight, or _color when paired with a valid prefix.
  • Custom CSS: A field named custom_css will be appended directly to the generated stylesheet.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How to Use OptionsBuilder — Step-by-step implementation guide.
  • UiBuilder API — Generic builder for CRUD and dashboards.
  • Theme Development — Integrating options into your themes.

docs.title: Page Type & Localization Class
docs.text: The Pages class is a specialized utility in GeniXCMS designed to identify the current content context within your theme's templates. It provides a semantic set of conditional methods that allow your design to adapt dynamically based on whether a visitor is viewing the homepage, a blog post, a static page, or a custom module.

docs.title: ⚡ Content Context Discovery
docs.text:
The following methods are the primary tool for theme logic, returning a bool based on the active route's detected type.

Method Returns true if current view is... Pages::isHome() The site's primary landing page (Index). Pages::isPost() An individual editorial blog post. Pages::isPage() A foundational static page (e.g., About Us). Pages::isCat() A category-based content archive. Pages::isTag() A tag-based content archive. Pages::isMod() A custom page provided by an active module.

docs.title: 🎨 Implementation Workflow
docs.text:
These context-aware methods are typically used in theme files (like sidebar.php, header.php, or footer.php) to toggle layouts or inject specialized meta-tags and assets.

// Example: Dynamic Sidebar Selection
if (Pages::isPost()) {
    // Inject blog-specific components (Author Bio, Related Posts)
    Theme::inc('sidebars/blog-meta'); 
} elseif (Pages::isPage()) {
    // Inject page-specific components (Table of contents, etc.)
    Theme::inc('sidebars/page-navigation');
}

docs.title: 🏗️ Technical Engine
docs.text:
The Pages class functions as a thin wrapper around the global $data array.

  • Initialization: The context is determined by the Control Class immediately after the Router resolves the URI.
  • Population: The framework populates the p_type key, which these methods monitor.

docs.title: 💡 Advanced Layout Switching
docs.text:
Combined with the Theme Class, you can create a completely polymorphic site architecture.

// In your theme's index.php
if (Pages::isHome()) {
    Theme::inc('layouts/home-hero');
} else {
    Theme::inc('layouts/subpage-header');
}

[!TIP] SEO Performance: Use Pages::isHome() in your header to decide whether to wrap your site name in an <h1> (on the home page) or an <a> (on sub-pages) for optimal semantic SEO hierarchy.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Theme Class — Dynamically including design components.
  • Router Class — How the system determines the page type from the URL.
  • User Guide: Pages — Managing static pages in the dashboard.

docs.title: PagesApi Class Interface
docs.text: The PagesApi class is a resource-specific controller within the GeniXCMS RESTful API framework. It handles standard CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) operations for Page resources via HTTP endpoints.

docs.title: Endpoint Mapping
docs.text:
The following HTTP methods are mapped to the class methods when accessing /api/v1/pages/:

Method Endpoint Action GET / List all active pages. GET /{id} Retrieve details of a specific page. POST / Create a new page. PUT /{id} Update an existing page. DELETE /{id} Permanently remove a page.

docs.title: index()
docs.text:
Handles retrieval of one or multiple page records.

Syntax: 

public function index($id = null)

  • If $id is null: Returns a list of all pages with status 1 and type page.
  • If $id is provided: Returns the specific page record or a 404 error if not found.

docs.title: submit()
docs.text: Processes a POST request to create a new page. Expects a JSON payload in the request body.

docs.title: update()
docs.text: Processes a PUT request to update specific fields of a page identified by its ID.

docs.title: delete()
docs.text: Processes a DELETE request to remove a page from the database.

docs.title: Usage in Modules
docs.text:
Modules can interact with this class internally if they need to bypass the standard Pages model logic or to extend the API behavior.

Example: Internal Execution 

$api = new PagesApi();
$result = $api->index(10); // Fetch page ID 10 via the API controller logic

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• RESTful API Reference — For authentication and global API response formats.
  • Pages Class Reference — For the underlying model used by this controller.

docs.title: Content Pagination Class
docs.text: The Paging class is a specialized utility in GeniXCMS designed to build structural pagination links for both the frontend content archives and the administrative management tables. It handles complex record counting, page calculation, and Bootstrap-compliant HTML generation in a single method call.

docs.title: Paging::create(array $vars, bool $smart_url = false)
docs.text:
Generates a complete, semantic HTML pagination fragment based on the provided configuration.

Parameter Type Default Description $vars array Required Associative configuration for record count and URL patterns. $smart_url bool false If true, it generates SEO-friendly URL segments (e.g., /paging/2/).

Returns: string (HTML fragment).

docs.title: ⚙️ Configuration Schema ($vars)
docs.text: Key Example Description table 'posts' Target database table to calculate total record volume. where 'status=1' Optional SQL WHERE clause for granular filtering. total 100 Optional manual count (overrides automated table query). max 10 Maximum items displayed per individual page. url Site::$url Base URL for the pagination assembly. paging 1 Current active page integer. type 'number' Display style: 'number' (pagination list) or 'pager' (Prev/Next).

docs.title: Professional Numbered List (Bootstrap)
docs.text:
Ideal for standard blog archives and administrative data grids.

$vars = [
    'table' => 'posts',
    'where' => "type='post' AND status='1'",
    'max'   => 12,
    'url'   => 'https://site.com/blog/',
    'paging'=> 1,
    'type'  => 'number'
];

echo Paging::create($vars, true);

docs.title: Simple "Previous/Next" Pager
docs.text:
Ideal for minimalist themes or sequential content flows.

$vars = [
    'table' => 'posts',
    'max'   => 20,
    'url'   => 'index.php?page=archives',
    'paging'=> 1,
    'type'  => 'pager'
];

echo Paging::create($vars);

docs.title: 🏗️ Technical Workflow
docs.text:
The Paging class operates through several automated phases:

  1. Metric Calculation: It performs a Db::num_rows() query on the provided table/where condition to determine the data universe size.
  2. Logic Partition: Calculates the total page count by dividing the universe size by the max value.
  3. URL Assembly: Decodes whether to use standard query parameters (?paging=X) or SEO-friendly segments (/paging/X/).
  4. HTML Synthesis: Built-in support for Bootstrap styling, injecting active and disabled CSS classes to individual page nodes.

[!TIP] SEO Performance: Always enable the $smart_url = true parameter for public-facing content lists. This ensures search engine crawlers can easily index your deep content archives through clean, predictable URL paths.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Router Class — How the system handles the /paging/ URL segment.
