dropdown_button Button with nested action links. label, items (array of links/headers/dividers) pagination Wrapper for pagination links. html (raw HTML) bulk_actions Table action selector and button. options (assoc array), form, button_label list_group Bootstrap list group with icons/badges. items (array of title/subtitle/icon/badge) alert Dismissible alert message box. content, style (info/success/danger), dismissible badge Small pill-shaped indicator. text, color, pill (bool) heading Section header with optional icon. text, subtitle, icon, class search_group Search input with button. name, action, placeholder, hidden (array) html / raw Direct raw HTML output. html

docs.title: ⚡ Reactivity & Dependencies (require)
docs.text: UiBuilder supports element dependencies, allowing you to show/hide or enable/disable elements based on the state of another element.

docs.title: Dependency Parameters
docs.text: Property Type Description require string The name or id of the target element to watch. require_value string The value that must be met for the dependency to trigger. Default: on. require_action string The action to perform: show (default) or enable.

docs.title: 1. Show field based on Select value
docs.text:
[
    'type' => 'select',
    'name' => 'payment_method',
    'label' => 'Payment Method',
    'options' => ['cash' => 'Cash', 'transfer' => 'Bank Transfer']
],
[
    'type' => 'input',
    'name' => 'bank_name',
    'label' => 'Bank Name',
    'require' => 'payment_method',
    'require_value' => 'transfer'
]

docs.title: 2. Enable input based on Checkbox
docs.text:
[
    'type' => 'checkbox',
    'name' => 'use_custom_discount',
    'label' => 'Use Custom Discount'
],
[
    'type' => 'input',
    'name' => 'discount_value',
    'label' => 'Discount Percentage',
    'require' => 'use_custom_discount',
    'require_value' => 'on',
    'require_action' => 'enable'
]

docs.title: 💡 Practical Pattern: The "Double Render"
docs.text:
If you need to wrap the UiBuilder inside a form or custom container, use the instance call within your backend controller:

// 1. Define Schema
$schema = [ ... ];

// 2. Initialize Builder
$builder = new UiBuilder($schema);

// 3. Render inside custom HTML
echo '<form method="POST" action="...">';
$builder->render(); 
echo '</form>';

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How to Use UiBuilder — Comprehensive guide on creating rows, cards, and tables.
  • OptionsBuilder API — Documentation for the specialized settings engine.

docs.title: UpdatesAjax Class
docs.text: The UpdatesAjax class provides a set of AJAX endpoints for checking for system updates, including the GeniXCMS core, installed modules, and themes. It also handles the orchestration of the update process.

docs.title: index($param = null)
docs.text:
The primary endpoint for update-related checks.

  • Endpoint: index.php?ajax=updates&token=TOKEN
  • Actions: check (default), perform_update, install_go_service.

Workflow (Check):

  1. Authentication: Calls Ajax::auth($param) to verify the token.
  2. Core Check: Fetches the latest core version from the official API.
  3. Inventory Scan: Scans the inc/mods/ and inc/themes/ directories for installed extensions.
  4. Marketplace Check: Sends a batch request to the marketplace API to check for updates for all installed modules and themes.
  5. Response: Returns a comprehensive JSON object with current and latest versions, download URLs, and a can_update boolean for each component.

docs.title: performUpdate($param = null)
docs.text:
Initiates the actual update process for the core or an extension.

  • Security: Only Super-admins (level 0) are permitted to perform core updates.
  • Workflow:
    1. Downloads the update ZIP package to assets/cache/.
    2. Performs a security scan using Security::scanZip().
    3. Extracts the package in-place.
  • Response: Returns a JSON object with the installation status.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Permissions
docs.text:
• Authentication: Requires a valid system token passed in the request.
  • Role-Based Access: Access is restricted based on the operation. Core updates are strictly limited to Super-admins.
  • External Requests: Uses the Http class to communicate with official GeniXCMS update servers with SSL verification bypass for maximum compatibility (though it logs the user agent).

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ajax Class — The main AJAX dispatcher.
  • System Class — To retrieve current version information.
  • Marketplace Class — For extension installation logic.
  • Http Class — For fetching external update data.

docs.title: File Ingestion & Upload Class
docs.text: The Upload class is the primary interface for handling secure file ingestion into GeniXCMS. It provides an abstraction layer over PHP's native move_uploaded_file system, adding critical features like strict extension filtering, automatic filename sanitization, and unique storage path generation.

docs.title: Upload::go(string $name, ...)
docs.text:
Processes a single file upload from an HTML form.

Parameter Type Default Description $name string Required The name attribute of the <input type="file"> field. $path string Required Destination folder relative to GX_PATH (e.g., assets/uploads/). $allowed array [] List of permitted extensions (e.g., ['jpg', 'png']). $unique bool false If true, creates a cryptographically unique filename.

Returns: array (Result/Metadata) or string (Error message if failed).

// Example: Processing a site logo upload
$valid_ext = ['jpg', 'png', 'svg', 'webp'];
$result = Upload::go('site_logo', 'assets/images/', $valid_ext, true);

if (isset($result['error'])) {
    System::alert(['alertDanger' => [$result['error']]]);
} else {
    $logo_url = $result['fileurl'];
}

docs.title: 📦 Result Metadata Schema
docs.text:
Upon a successful upload, the engine returns a detailed metadata object for immediate use or database persistence:

  • filename: The final, sanitized name of the file (including any unique prefixes).
  • filesize: The exact volume in bytes.
  • path: The relative storage path (e.g., assets/images/logo.png).
  • filepath: The absolute server filesystem path.
  • fileurl: The derived public-facing URL resolved via the Url Class.

docs.title: 🛡️ Integrated Security Features
docs.text:
Protection is baked into every phase of the upload lifecycle:

  1. Strict Whitelisting: The system ignores MIME types provided by the client and relies exclusively on your provided $allowed extensions list.
  2. Naming Sanitization: Filenames are processed through Typo::slugify() and Typo::cleanX() to remove spaces, special characters, and potential script injection points.
  3. Unique Collisions: Enabling $unique prevents overwriting existing files and makes user-uploaded assets unguessable for external scanners.
  4. Directory Integrity: The system automatically attempts to create the target $path if it does not already exist.

[!IMPORTANT] Server Limits: File uploads are subject to your PHP upload_max_filesize and post_max_size configuration. If a large file disappears during upload, check these settings in your php.ini.

[!CAUTION] Extension Spoofing: Always include your own server-side validation. While the GeniXCMS Upload class checks extensions, for high-security environments, consider the Files Class to verify actual file headers and content integrity.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Files Class — Advanced media header and checksum verification.
  • Image Class — Optimizing and resizing images after upload.
  • Url Class — How the public upload URL is calculated.

docs.title: Url Class API Reference
docs.text: The Url class is the central routing and path generation engine in GeniXCMS. it handles the creation of all system links, ensuring they comply with either standard query-string formats or "Smart URL" (friendly permalink) formats.

docs.title: Url::post(int|string $id): string
docs.text:
Generates a full URL for a specific post.

  • Hook: Applies the post_url filter.
  • Smart URL: https://site.com/post-slug/
  • Standard: https://site.com/?post=123

docs.title: Url::page(string $slug): string
docs.text:
Generates a full URL for a static page.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/page-slug/
  • Standard: https://site.com/?page=page-slug

docs.title: Url::cat(int $id): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for a category archive.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/category/12/category-name/

docs.title: Url::ajax(string $resource, string|array $action = '', array $params = []): string
docs.text:
Generates a secure internal Ajax endpoint URL.

  • $resource: The Ajax class/resource to target.
  • $action: Specific action to perform (or array of params).
  • Security: Automatically includes the system TOKEN.
  • Smart URL: https://site.com/ajax/resource/TOKEN/?action=save

docs.title: Url::api(string $resource, string $id = '', array $params = []): string
docs.text:
Generates a GeniXCMS RESTful API endpoint URL.

  • $resource: API resource name (e.g., 'posts').
  • $id: Specific resource identifier/ID.
  • Smart URL: https://site.com/api/v1/posts/123

docs.title: Url::mod(string $name, string $act = '', array $params = []): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for a module backend.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/mod/module-name/?act=edit&id=1

docs.title: Url::thumb(string $src, string $type = '', string $size = '', string $align = ''): string
docs.text:
Generates a dynamic thumbnail path using the ThumbFly engine.

  • Parameters:
    • type: crop or resize.
    • size: e.g., 400x300.
    • align: e.g., center.
  • Note: Strips duplicate thumb prefixes and automatically uses Site::$cdn.

docs.title: Url::theme(): string
docs.text: Returns the base URL path of the currently active theme (e.g., inc/themes/my-theme/).

docs.title: Url::custom(string $url): string
docs.text: Returns a custom URL as provided. Simple passthrough utility.

docs.title: Url::sitemap(string $section = null): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for the sitemap (XML).

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/sitemap/posts.xml
  • Standard: https://site.com/index.php?page=sitemap

docs.title: Url::rss(): string
docs.text:
Generates the URL for the main RSS feed.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/rss/
  • Standard: https://site.com/?rss

docs.title: Url::tag(string $name): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for a specific tag archive.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/tag/my-tag/
  • Standard: https://site.com/?tag=my-tag

docs.title: Url::user(string $username, string $section = ''): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for a frontend user profile page.

  • $section: Optional profile section (e.g., 'settings', 'orders').
  • Smart URL: https://site.com/user/johndoe/settings/
  • Standard: https://site.com/?user=johndoe&section=settings

docs.title: Url::author(string $username, string $type = ''): string
docs.text:
Generates a URL for an author's public profile/archive.

  • Smart URL: https://site.com/author/johndoe/

docs.title: Url::search(): string
docs.text: Generates the URL for the search results page.

docs.title: Url::slug(int $id): string
docs.text: Internal utility to retrieve the URL slug for a specific post ID from the database.

docs.title: Url::current(): string
docs.text: Returns the complete URL of the page currently being viewed (including protocol, host, and request URI).

docs.title: Url::breadcrumbs(array $data = []): string
docs.text:
Generates standardized Bootstrap/Tailwind-ready HTML breadcrumbs.

  • Hook: Applies the breadcrumbs_filter.

docs.title: Url::archive(int $month, int $year): string
docs.text: Generates a URL for a specific time-based archive.

docs.title: Url::flag(string $lang): string
docs.text: Generates a language switcher link for the given language code.

docs.title: Url::login(string $params = ''): string / Url::logout(): string
docs.text: Generates URLs for the system authentication pages.

docs.title: ⚡ Smart URL logic
docs.text:
The behavior of this class is globally controlled by the SMART_URL constant (defined in gxadmin/inc/config.php or Options).

  • If permalink_use_index_php is enabled, all Smart URLs will automatically include index.php/ in the path for servers without URL rewriting support.
  • If multilang_enable is active, the class automatically injects the active language prefix (e.g., /en/) into the generated paths.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Programmable Custom URLs — How to define your own routing patterns.
  • RESTful API Guide — Using API endpoints generated by Url::api().

docs.title: Identity & Authentication Class
docs.text: The User class is the central security and identity management engine for GeniXCMS. It handles the entire user lifecycle, from secure registration and role-based access control (RBAC) to profile management, session persistence, and multi-level administrative permissions.

docs.title: 🔐 Authentication & Session Security
docs.text: GeniXCMS implements a robust session-based security layer linked to the Session Class.

docs.title: User::secure()
docs.text: The primary middleware for protected pages. It verifies the active user session; if no session is detected, it automatically redirects the visitor to the login page with an encoded backto return URL.

docs.title: User::isLoggedin()
docs.text: Returns a bool indicating if the visitor is currently authenticated within the system.

docs.title: 🛡️ Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS uses a tiered numeric level system. Access is granted if the user's level is equal to or lower (smaller numeric value) than the requirement.

Level Role Description 0 Administrator Full system-wide orchestration. 1 Supervisor Site-wide management without kernel access. 2 Editor Content curation and comment moderation. 3 Author Authoring activities and personal content management. 4 Contributor Can submit content for review. 5 VIP Member Premium member level. 6 General Member Default level for registered subscribers.

docs.title: User::access(string $lvl)
docs.text:
Checks if the current user meets the specified clearance level.

// Only allow Editors and above
if (User::access('2')) {
    echo "Access granted to editorial tools.";
}

docs.title: User::create(array $data) / User::update(array $data)
docs.text:
Standardized methods for committing user records to the database.

  • Data Encryption: Passwords are automatically hashed using the current system-wide encryption algorithms (e.g., PASSWORD_BCRYPT).
  • Validation: Ensures unique User IDs and Email addresses during the creation process.

docs.title: 📂 Identity Retrieval Helpers
docs.text:
Quickly resolve specific user attributes using their unique ID or Username.

  • User::avatar($id): Resolves the user's profile image or Gravatar based on site settings.
  • User::email($id): Fetches the unique contact address for a specific user ID.
  • User::group($id): Returns the plain-text role name associated with the user's numeric level.

[!IMPORTANT] Password Security: Never attempt to manually hash passwords using md5() or sha1(). Always use the User::create() and User::update() methods, which leverage modern, salt-aware PHP password hashing functions.

[!TIP] Custom Redirects: The User::secure() method is typically called at the top of protected controller files to prevent unauthorized execution of your business logic.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Session Class — How user states are persistently stored.
  • Acl Class — Managing fine-grained permission matrices.
  • Users Guide — Managing accounts from the dashboard.

docs.title: UserAjax Class
docs.text: The UserAjax class provides asynchronous access to the system's user database. It is primarily used to populate the administrative dashboard's user management table with advanced filtering and search capabilities.

docs.title: list_users($param = null)
docs.text:
Retrieves a paginated list of users based on the provided query parameters.

  • URL Parameters:
    • num (int): Number of users per page (default: 10).
    • offset (int): Number of users to skip (default: 0).
    • q (string): Search term to filter by username or email.
    • group (int): Filter by user group (access level).
    • status (int): Filter by user status (1 for active, 0 for inactive).

Response Structure:

  • status: 'success' or 'error'.
  • headers: An array of column headers for the dashboard table.
  • data: An array of user row objects processed via User::getDashboardRow().
  • total: Total number of matching users.
  • limit: The current page size.
  • offset: The current starting offset.

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Authentication
docs.text:
• Authentication: Requires a valid system token passed in the request. The class calls Ajax::auth($param) internally.
  • Unauthorized Access: Returns a 401 Unauthorized error if the token is invalid or missing.
  • XSS Protection: All query parameters are sanitized using Typo::int() and Typo::cleanX().
  • Joins: Automatically joins with the user_detail table to include country information in the dashboard view.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ajax Class — The main AJAX dispatcher.
  • User Class — The core user management engine.
  • Token Class — Handling security tokens.

docs.title: UserProfile Class
docs.text: The UserProfile class is the central orchestrator for the frontend user dashboard. It handles profile routing, dynamic profile tabs (sections), data retrieval, settings forms, and injecting custom elements into the user dropdown menus. By leveraging this class, Themes and Modules can extend the user dashboard without hacking core templates.

docs.title: UserProfile::registerSection
docs.text:
Registers a new tab/section into the user profile dashboard. This generates a frontend URL automatically, e.g. /user/username/finance.

Signature: 

public static function registerSection(string $key, array $config): void

Parameters:

  • $key (string) — The unique URL slug for the section (e.g. 'finance', 'orders').
  • $config (array) — An array containing configuration options:
    • 'label' (string) — The display label on the frontend.
    • 'icon' (string) — Material Symbols icon name.
    • 'callback' (callable) — A function that returns HTML string output for this section.
    • 'min_group' (int|string) — The minimum group level required. Admin = 0, Member = 6.
    • 'own_only' (bool) — If true, the section is only visible to the profile owner.
    • 'order' (int) — Sorting index.

docs.title: UserProfile::registerDropdownItem
docs.text:
Registers a programmatic item to be rendered inside the global User Dropdown navigation menu (in the header). You can insert navigation links or graphical dividers.

Signature: 

public static function registerDropdownItem(string $key, array $config): void

Parameters:

  • $key (string) — A unique identifier.
  • $config (array) — Configuration parameters:
    • 'label' (string) — Menu text.
    • 'url' (string) — Target hyperlink URL.
    • 'icon' (string) — Material symbols icon.
    • 'min_group' (int) — Privilege constraint.
    • 'order' (int) — Sorting sequence.
    • 'divider' (bool) — Renders an <hr> equivalent instead of a link.
    • 'danger' (bool) — Styles the text/icon in red (e.g., Delete/Logout).

docs.title: UserProfile::getSections
docs.text:
Returns all registered and permitted sections for the active session context.

Signature: 

public static function getSections(string $profileUsername): array

docs.title: UserProfile::getDropdownItems
docs.text:
Returns the array of navigation structures intended for drop-down menus, filtered by user privileges.

Signature: 

public static function getDropdownItems(): array

docs.title: UserProfile::renderSection
docs.text:
Executes the associated callback function for a given section and outputs its HTML.

Signature: 

public static function renderSection(string $key, string $username, object $user): string

docs.title: UserProfile::getDetailData & UserProfile::getPublicData
docs.text:
Retrieves standardized user schemas for the authenticated user, wrapping properties such as addresses, locations, joins dates, and system states.

Signature: 

public static function getDetailData(string $username): ?object
public static function getPublicData(string $username): ?object

docs.title: 🎨 Theme Implementation
docs.text: Themes can integrate the user profile system in two ways: using the built-in orchestrator or building a completely custom UI.

docs.title: 1. Using the Built-in Orchestrator (Recommended)
docs.text:
The fastest way to support the user profile system is to call UserProfile::renderPage($data) within your theme's user profile template (e.g., user-profile.latte).

// In your theme's .latte file
{!UserProfile::renderPage($data)}

This method generates a professional, responsive layout including: - Sidebar: Avatar, Display Name, Username, Role Badge, and Navigation Tabs. - Notification Bar: Success/Error messages for form submissions. - Main Content: Renders the active section (Profile, Settings, or custom tabs).

docs.title: 2. Custom UI Implementation
docs.text:
If you need a unique design, you can build your own layout using the data provided in the $data array and UserProfile helper methods.

// Accessing profile data manually
$user = UserProfile::getDetailData($profile_username);
$sections = UserProfile::getSections($profile_username);

// Rendering the avatar
$avatar = UserProfile::avatar($user->email, 120);

docs.title: 🚀 Extending from Themes (function.php)
docs.text: You can register custom tabs and menu items directly from your theme's function.php.

docs.title: Registering a Custom Tab
docs.text:
UserProfile::registerSection('membership', [
    'label'    => 'Membership Info',
    'icon'     => 'workspace_premium',
    'callback' => 'MyTheme::renderMembershipTab',
    'order'    => 30,
]);

// Callback method
class MyTheme {
    public static function renderMembershipTab($username, $user) {
        return '<div class="card">Your membership expires on ...</div>';
    }
}

docs.title: Adding to the Header Dropdown
docs.text:
UserProfile::registerDropdownItem('my_rewards', [
    'label' => 'My Rewards',
    'url'   => Url::custom('/rewards'),
    'icon'  => 'redeem',
    'order' => 25
]);

docs.title: ⚓ Available Hooks
docs.text: Hook Type Description user_profile_dropdown Filter Modify the dropdown menu items array. user_profile_sections Filter Modify the registered profile tabs array. user_profile_section_{key} Filter Intercept and override the HTML for a specific section. user_profile_overview_extra Filter Append HTML to the default "Profile Overview" tab. user_profile_settings_fields Filter Append custom input fields to the "Account Settings" form. user_profile_settings_save_action Action Run logic after the settings form is saved.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Url Class — Generating profile URLs with Url::user().
  • User Class — Core user management and authentication.
  • Customizing User Profiles — Advanced guide on dashboard extensions.

docs.title: Vendor Management Class
docs.text: The Vendor class is the central orchestrator for third-party libraries within GeniXCMS. It primarily manages the integration of Composer-based packages and legacy non-PSR libraries, ensuring that external dependencies are available, secured, and properly mapped to both internal paths and public URLs.

docs.title: Vendor::autoload()
docs.text:
This method manually triggers the inclusion of the main Composer autoload.php. It is called internally by the system kernel to prepare the environment for third-party classes.

// Typically called once during system boot
Vendor::autoload();

docs.title: Vendor::loadonce(string $library_path)
docs.text:
For libraries that do not support PSR-4 autoloading or aren't managed via Composer's main manifest, use loadonce() to include them safely from the inc/lib/Vendor/ directory.

// Example: Manually loading a specific library component
Vendor::loadonce('tcpdf/tcpdf.php');

docs.title: 🔗 Path & URL Resolution
docs.text:
Managing assets from vendor packages requires consistent URL and path generation.

Method Return Value Common Use Case Vendor::url() string Including vendor CSS/JS in themes or modules (e.g., Bootstrap, FontAwesome). Vendor::path() string Reading library configuration files or checking for file existence on the server.

docs.title: 📦 Integrated Core Libraries
docs.text: GeniXCMS comes pre-packaged with several industry-standard libraries managed through the Vendor layer:

docs.title: 🛡️ Security & Content
docs.text:
• HTMLPurifier: The gold standard for sanitizing user-generated HTML and preventing XSS.
  • Latte: The primary templating engine featuring automated contextual escaping.
  • Summernote: Lightweight WYSIWYG editor for the content dashboard.

docs.title: 📧 Communication & Media
docs.text:
• PHPMailer: Enterprise-grade email transmission engine.
  • Intervention Image: High-level API for dynamic image processing, cropping, and WebP conversion.
  • GuzzleHttp: Robust HTTP client for handling remote API requests.

[!TIP] Extending Vendors: If you are adding a new library via Composer, make sure to run composer update in the inc/lib/ directory to update the vendor autoloader, then call Vendor::autoload() within your module or theme logic.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Security Class — How vendors like HTMLPurifier are used for data sanitization.
  • Vite Helper — Managing modern frontend assets alongside legacy vendor libraries.

docs.title: Vite Helper Class
docs.text: The Vite class is a specialized utility designed to bridge the gap between GeniXCMS and the Vite asset bundling ecosystem. It enables high-performance frontend development workflows, including Hot Module Replacement (HMR) and automated production asset management.

docs.title: 🛠️ Configuration Defaults
docs.text: Key Default Value Description Dev Server http://localhost:5173 The default URL where the Vite development server runs. Main Entry assets/src/main.js The primary JavaScript file to be injected. Style Entry assets/src/style.scss The root style file (SCSS/CSS) for the theme.

docs.title: static Vite::isDev()
docs.text:
Determines the current operational mode of the asset pipeline.

  • Storage: Driven by the vite_dev_mode option in the database.
  • States: Returns true if set to on, otherwise false.
  • Primary Use: Used internally by client() to switch between HMR and Manifest loading.

docs.title: static Vite::client($entry = 'assets/src/main.js')
docs.text: The primary injector for your theme's head or footer. It intelligently handles asset loading based on your environment.

docs.title: 🔄 Development Mode (HMR)
docs.text: When active, it injects the following into your HTML: 1. Vite Client: The @vite/client runtime for real-time updates. 2. Entry Point: Your source file (e.g., main.js) served directly from the dev server.

docs.title: 🚀 Production Mode (Manifest)
docs.text: When inactive, it follows this sequence: 1. Lookup: Accesses assets/dist/manifest.json. 2. JS Injection: Loads the versioned/hashed JavaScript bundle. 3. CSS Injection: Automatically discovers and injects any associated CSS chunks mapped to that entry point.

docs.title: static Vite::setDevServer($url)
docs.text:
Overrides the default development server address. Use this if you are running Vite on a custom port or within a specific container.

// Example: Custom port configuration
Vite::setDevServer('http://localhost:3000');

docs.title: 📂 Implementation in Themes
docs.text:
Integrating Vite into your theme requires only a single call in your header.php or footer.php:

// Load assets dynamically
Vite::client('assets/src/main.js');

[!TIP] Performance First: In production mode, the Vite class ensures that only the necessary versioned assets are loaded, preventing cache-busting issues and reducing page weight.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• How to Use Vite — Step-by-step guide for setting up your first Vite theme.
  • Options Class — Learn how to toggle vite_dev_mode via code.

docs.title: Aspect-Based Widget & Sidebar Class
docs.text: The Widget class is the primary engine for rendering dynamic, reusable content blocks (Widgets) across various theme locations. It provides a standardized framework for both core system widgets (e.g., Recent Posts, Tag Cloud) and custom module-driven components, ensuring a consistent visual architecture throughout your platform.

docs.title: ⚡ Active Rendering Logic
docs.text: GeniXCMS widgets are location-aware. They are retrieved and rendered based on their assigned region in the admin dashboard.

docs.title: Widget::show(string $location)
docs.text:
The primary entry point for theme integration.

  • Process: Fetches all active records assigned to the specified $location (e.g., sidebar, footer_1), sorts them by their manual priority, and returns the aggregated HTML.
  • Implementation: Typically called within a theme's sidebar.php or footer.php.
{* In your theme's sidebar.tpl *}
<aside class="col-md-4 main-sidebar">
    {Widget::show('sidebar')|noescape}
</aside>

docs.title: Widget::addLocation(string $id, string $name)
docs.text:
(Since 2.0.0) Registers a custom destination slot for widgets programmatically. This enables theme developers to expose specific template locations to the admin dashboard.

  • Implementation: Typically called within your theme's function.php or a module's initialization.
// Register new widget locations inside your theme
Widget::addLocation('header_top', 'Top Header Area');
Widget::addLocation('sidebar_left', 'Sidebar Left (Secondary)');

docs.title: Widget::getLocations()
docs.text:
Retrieves all registered widget locations. Returns an associative array [id => name]. This method automatically invokes the widget_locations hook, allowing plugins to intercept and modify the available regions dynamically:

Hooks::attach('widget_locations', function($locations) {
    $locations['custom_slot'] = 'My Custom Slot';
    return $locations;
});

docs.title: 🏗️ Supported Content Types
docs.text:
The Widget engine automatically resolves various content sources into standardized HTML modules:

Type Description Recent Posts Automatically fetches the 5 most recent articles with thumbnails. Recent Comments Lists newest reader interactions for social proof. Tag Cloud Injects a frequency-weighted list of site-wide tags. Static Content Renders raw HTML or text provided via the admin editor. Module Hook Dynamically triggers a specified Hook to pull data from a third-party module.

docs.title: 🎨 Professional UI Blueprint
docs.text:
Every widget is wrapped in a standardized, Bootstrap-compliant container to ensure design consistency across all themes.

<div class="widget-box mb-4" id="widget-12">
    <div class="widget-header">
        <h3 class="widget-title h5 fw-bold m-0 text-uppercase">
            <i class="bi bi-star-fill me-2"></i> Widget Title
        </h3>
    </div>
    <div class="widget-body">
        <!-- Widget Dynamic Content ... -->
    </div>
</div>

docs.title: 🏹 Management Workflow
docs.text:
• Drag & Drop: Reorder widgets in the dashboard under Appearance > Widgets.
  • Location Mapping: Assign widgets to specific theme slots (e.g., Header, Main Sidebar, Post Footer).
  • Configuration: Edit the internal title and content (plain text or HTML) for each instance.

[!TIP] Custom Module Widgets: To create a widget for your module, use the Custom Module type and specify a unique hook name. Then, in your module's function.php, use Hooks::attach('your_hook', 'your_render_function') to inject your own HTML.

[!IMPORTANT] Styling Consistency: Ensure your theme's CSS targets the .widget-box, .widget-header, and .widget-body classes to maintain a premium, unified aesthetic for the entire sidebar subsystem.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — How module-driven widgets are registered.
  • Theme Class — Integrating widget locations into your templates.
  • Widgets Guide — Managing your site's sidebars in the dashboard.

docs.title: Bot Protection (reCAPTCHA) Class
docs.text: The Xaptcha class is a specialized security wrapper for Google reCAPTCHA v2. It provides GeniXCMS with a streamlined, high-level interface for implementing bot protection across sensitive forms — including comments, registrations, and administrative logins — ensuring your site remains secure from automated spam and brute-force attacks.

docs.title: ⚡ Global Configuration
docs.text:
CAPTCHA policies are managed centrally via the Settings > General dashboard.

Parameter Identifier Description Enable google_captcha_enable Global toggle (on/off) for the entire system. Site Key google_captcha_sitekey Your public public-facing key from Google Console. Secret Key google_captcha_secret Your private server-side verification key. Language google_captcha_lang The ISO language code for the widget (e.g., en, id).

docs.title: Xaptcha::html()
docs.text:
Generates and injects the complete reCAPTCHA widget logic.

  • Process: Automatically includes the Google JavaScript library from the global CDN and renders the <div class="g-recaptcha"> container with your configured Site Key.
  • Safety: Returns an empty string if CAPTCHA is disabled, allowing for transparent theme integration.

docs.title: Xaptcha::verify(string $response)
docs.text:
Performs a secure server-to-server handshake with Google's API to validate the submission.

// Typically executed within a form controller
$token = $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'];
if (Xaptcha::verify($token)) {
    // Human visitor verified
}

docs.title: 📝 Implementation Pattern
docs.text: Use the following pattern to secure your module's forms:

docs.title: Frontend (Latte / PHP Template)
docs.text:
{* In your theme's register.tpl *}
<div class="form-group mb-3">
    {if Xaptcha::isEnable()}
        {Xaptcha::html()|noescape}
    {/if}
</div>

docs.title: Backend (PHP Controller)
docs.text:
if (Xaptcha::isEnable()) {
    $captcha = $_POST['g-recaptcha-response'] ?? '';
    if (!Xaptcha::verify($captcha)) {
        System::alert(['alertDanger' => [_('Bot detected! Please complete the CAPTCHA.')]]);
        return;
    }
}

[!CAUTION] API Key Mismatch: If you see "Error: Invalid site key" in the widget, ensure that the domain name in your Google Cloud Console exactly matches your Site::$url.

[!IMPORTANT] Privacy Compliance: Google reCAPTCHA tracks user behavior to distinguish humans from bots. Ensure your site's Privacy Policy includes a notice regarding the use of this service to remain compliant with GDPR and other regulations.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Token Class — How CSRF protection works alongside CAPTCHA.
  • Security Settings — Managing site-wide protection layers.
  • General Settings Guide — Configuring your Google API keys.

docs.title: Admin Themes
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.4.0 introduces a pluggable admin theme system. The admin panel layout is no longer fixed — you can switch between built-in themes or create your own.

docs.title: Selecting a Theme
docs.text:
Set the ADMIN_THEME constant in inc/config/config.php:

!defined('ADMIN_THEME') ? define('ADMIN_THEME', 'moluka') : null;

docs.title: Built-in Themes
docs.text: Name Description Accent default Classic sidebar layout with Bootstrap Blue #3b82f6 moluka Clean SaaS-style, white sidebar, avatar, treeview nav #00A3EA fresco Compact financial-dashboard style, section labels Lime #c8f04a ribbon MS Office-style ribbon toolbar, tabs per menu item #2563eb

docs.title: Theme Structure
docs.text:
Admin themes live in gxadmin/themes/{name}/:

gxadmin/themes/
└── mytheme/
    ├── header.php       ← Main layout: <html>, <head>, sidebar, topbar, opens content
    ├── footer.php       ← Closes content, footer, scripts, </body></html>
    ├── headermini.php   ← Minimal layout for no-access / error pages
    ├── footermini.php   ← Closes headermini
    ├── auth/            ← (optional) login, register, forgotpass templates
    └── install/         ← (optional) installation wizard templates

Files missing from your theme automatically fall back to default — you only need to override what you change.

docs.title: 1. Create the folder
docs.text: gxadmin/themes/mytheme/

docs.title: 2. Write header.php
docs.text:
The header must: - Output a valid <!DOCTYPE html> and <head> with <?= Site::loadLibHeader(); ?> - Open the main content wrapper - Call <?php echo Hooks::run('admin_header_action'); ?> inside <head>

Minimal example:

<?php defined('GX_LIB') or die('Direct Access Not Allowed!'); ?>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
    <?= Site::meta(['backend']); ?>
    <?= Site::loadLibHeader(); ?>
    <?php echo Hooks::run('admin_header_action'); ?>
    <?php if (!defined('OFFLINE_MODE') || !OFFLINE_MODE): ?>
    <link href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;600;700&display=swap" rel="stylesheet">
    <?php endif; ?>
</head>
<body>
    <!-- your sidebar / topbar HTML -->
    <div id="main-content">

docs.title: 3. Write footer.php
docs.text:
</div><!-- /main-content -->
    <footer>...</footer>
    <?= Site::loadLibFooter(); ?>
    <?php echo Hooks::run('admin_footer_action', $data ?? []); ?>
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 4. Activate
docs.text:
// inc/config/config.php
!defined('ADMIN_THEME') ? define('ADMIN_THEME', 'mytheme') : null;

docs.title: Available Hooks
docs.text:
Use these hooks to inject content into any admin theme without modifying theme files:

Hook Position admin_header_action Inside <head> admin_footer_action Before </body> admin_header_top_right_action Top-right of the header bar admin_sidebar_start Top of sidebar nav admin_sidebar_end Bottom of sidebar nav

docs.title: Ribbon-specific hooks
docs.text: Hook Position admin_ribbon_home Home tab panel admin_ribbon_content Content tab panel admin_ribbon_tab_{id} Any tab by menu item ID

docs.title: Active Detection Helpers
docs.text:
All built-in themes use a strict URL matching helper to highlight the active menu item. When building your own theme, use the same pattern:

// Parse all params from child URL
parse_str(parse_url($child['url'] ?? '', PHP_URL_QUERY) ?? '', $cp);
$defined = array_filter($cp, fn($v) => $v !== '');

$isActive = true;
foreach ($defined as $k => $v) {
    $cur = match($k) {
        'page' => $_GET['page'] ?? '',
        'mod'  => $_GET['mod']  ?? '',
        'sel'  => $_GET['sel']  ?? '',
        'act'  => $_GET['act']  ?? '',
        'type' => $_GET['type'] ?? '',
        default => $_GET[$k] ?? '',
    };
    if ($cur !== $v) { $isActive = false; break; }
}
// Negative guards: if child URL has no 'type', current must also have none
if (!isset($defined['type'])) {
    $t = $_GET['type'] ?? '';
    if ($t !== '' && $t !== 'post') $isActive = false;
}
if (!isset($defined['mod']) && ($_GET['mod'] ?? '') !== '') $isActive = false;
if (!isset($defined['act']) && ($_GET['act'] ?? '') !== '') $isActive = false;
if (!isset($defined['sel']) && ($_GET['sel'] ?? '') !== '') $isActive = false;

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Asset Management
  • System Configuration
  • Admin Menu API

docs.title: Asset Management with Asset Class
docs.text: The Asset class in GeniXCMS is a modern replacement for manually echoing script and link tags in hooks. It provides a centralized registry for CSS and JS files, simplifies dependency management, and supports context-aware loading (frontend vs. backend).

docs.title: 1. Registering Assets
docs.text: Use Asset::register() to inform the system about a file. You can register assets from a module's init() or a theme's constructor.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
Asset::register(
    string $id, 
    string $type, 
    string $url, 
    string $pos = 'header', 
    array $deps = [], 
    int $priority = 20, 
    string $context = 'all'
);
  • $id: A unique identifier (e.g., bootstrap, my-custom-js).
  • $type: css, js, or raw.
  • $url: The full URL to the file.
  • $pos: header or footer.
  • $deps: Array of dependency IDs (e.g., ['jquery']).
  • $priority: Execution order (lower runs first).
  • $context: admin, frontend, or all. This determines where the asset is allowed to load.

docs.title: Example: Registering from a Module
docs.text:
public static function init() {
    $siteUrl = Site::$url;
    // CSS for admin only
    Asset::register('my-mod-css', 'css', $siteUrl . '/inc/mod/my-mod/assets/style.css', 'header', [], 20, 'admin');

    // JS for both frontend and backend
    Asset::register('my-mod-js', 'js', $siteUrl . '/inc/mod/my-mod/assets/script.js', 'footer', ['jquery'], 25, 'all');
}

docs.title: 2. Enqueueing Assets
docs.text:
Once registered, an asset must be "enqueued" to be rendered in the current request.

// Enqueue by ID
Asset::enqueue('my-mod-js');

// Enqueue multiple
Asset::enqueue(['my-mod-css', 'bootstrap']);

If an asset is enqueued with a context that doesn't match the current environment (e.g., an admin asset enqueued on the frontend), the Asset manager will automatically skip it.

docs.title: 3. Dependency Management
docs.text: The Asset manager automatically resolves dependencies. If you enqueue an asset that depends on jquery, the system will ensure jquery is rendered before your script, even if jquery wasn't explicitly enqueued.

docs.title: Pre-defined Core Assets
docs.text: ID Type Version jquery JS 3.7.1 bootstrap JS/CSS 5.3.2 font-awesome CSS 6.5.1 bi CSS Bootstrap Icons 1.11.3

docs.title: 4. Context-Aware Loading
docs.text:
GeniXCMS 2.0 introduces explicit contexts to prevent admin-specific scripts from bloat-loading on the frontend.

  • admin: Only loads if GX_ADMIN is defined as true.
  • frontend: Only loads if GX_ADMIN is not defined or false.
  • all: Always allowed to load.

docs.title: 5. Integrating with Themes
docs.text: Themes must call the following methods in their header and footer templates to output enqueued assets. If your theme uses Latte (which is recommended), you must use the |noescape filter to prevent HTML entities from being escaped.

docs.title: header.php / header.latte
docs.text:
Native PHP: 

<head>
    ...
    <?= Site::loadLibHeader(); ?>
</head>
Latte Template: 
<head>
    ...
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>

docs.title: footer.php / footer.latte
docs.text:
Native PHP: 

    ...
    <?= Site::loadLibFooter(); ?>
</body>
Latte Template: 
    ...
    {Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>

Note: These methods now return the generated HTML strings instead of echoing them directly, ensuring that metadata and layouts maintain the correct sequence.

docs.title: 6. Raw Assets
docs.text:
You can also register "raw" HTML blocks (like inline <script> or <style> tags).

$rawHtml = '<script>console.log("Hello GeniXCMS!");</script>';
Asset::register('my-inline-js', 'raw', $rawHtml, 'footer', [], 100);
Asset::enqueue('my-inline-js');

docs.title: 7. Offline Mode (v2.4.0+)
docs.text: GeniXCMS supports a fully offline-capable admin panel. When OFFLINE_MODE=true in config.php, all framework assets are served from local files instead of CDN.

docs.title: Enabling Offline Mode
docs.text:
// inc/config/config.php
!defined('OFFLINE_MODE') ? define('OFFLINE_MODE', true) : null;

docs.title: What Gets Localized
docs.text: Asset Local Path jQuery 3.7.1 assets/js/vendor/jquery-3.7.1.min.js jQuery UI 1.13.2 assets/js/vendor/jquery-ui-1.13.2.min.js Bootstrap 5.3.3 CSS assets/css/vendor/bootstrap-5.3.3.min.css Bootstrap 5.3.3 JS assets/js/vendor/bootstrap-5.3.3.bundle.min.js Bootstrap Icons 1.11.3 assets/css/vendor/bootstrap-icons-1.11.3.min.css Font Awesome 6.7.2 assets/fonts/fontawesome/css/all.min.css Inter Font (300–900) assets/fonts/inter/inter-*.woff2 Chart.js 4.4.1 assets/js/vendor/chartjs-4.4.1.min.js jsVectorMap 1.5.3 assets/js/vendor/jsvectormap-1.5.3.min.js EditorJS + tools assets/js/vendor/editorjs/

docs.title: Registering Your Own Offline-Aware Assets
docs.text:
$offline = defined('OFFLINE_MODE') && OFFLINE_MODE;
$siteUrl = rtrim(Site::$url, '/');

Asset::register(
    'my-lib',
    'js',
    $offline
        ? $siteUrl . '/assets/js/vendor/my-lib.min.js'
        : 'https://cdn.example.com/my-lib.min.js',
    'footer',
    [],
    20,
    'all'
);

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Create a Theme
  • Create a Module
  • Admin Themes
  • System Hooks

docs.title: Creating Modular Widget Blocks
docs.text: This guide explains how to create dynamic, modular widget blocks in GeniXCMS using the programmable Params System and the Widget API.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
In GeniXCMS, widgets are no longer limited to simple HTML or static module hooks. You can now define widgets with Modular Parameters—custom configuration fields that appear dynamically in the admin panel when a specific widget type is selected.

External developers can register their own widget types and handle the rendering entirely through hooks.

docs.title: 1. Registering a Custom Widget Type
docs.text:
To add your own widget type to the admin dropdown, use the widget_types_list filter hook. This ensures your widget is available in both the "Add Widget" and "Edit Widget" forms.

// Inside your module's init or function.php
Hooks::attach('widget_types_list', function($types) {
    $types['my_custom_widget'] = 'My Premium Widget';
    return $types;
});

You can also set custom metadata (like icons) using: 

Widget::setTypeLabel('my_custom_widget', [
    'title' => 'My Premium Widget',
    'icon'  => 'bi bi-star-fill'
]);

docs.title: 2. Defining Modular Parameters
docs.text:
Use the Params class to define which configuration fields should appear for your widget type. These fields appear instantly via AJAX when your type is selected.

Params::register([
    'widget' => [
        [
            'widget_type' => 'my_custom_widget', // Matches the ID above
            'grouptitle'  => 'Widget Settings',
            'icon'        => 'bi bi-gear-fill',
            'fields'      => [
                [
                    'name'  => 'widget_bg',
                    'title' => 'Background Color',
                    'type'  => 'color',
                    'default' => '#ffffff'
                ],
                [
                    'name'  => 'items_limit',
                    'title' => 'Number of Items',
                    'type'  => 'number',
                    'default' => '5'
                ]
            ]
        ]
    ]
]);

docs.title: 3. Handling Rendering
docs.text:
Instead of relying on the "Module Hook" field, you should use the dynamic rendering hook: widget_render_{type}. The GeniXCMS core will automatically look for this hook if your widget type is active.

// The hook matches your registered widget_type ID
Hooks::attach('widget_render_my_custom_widget', function($w) {
    // $w is the widget object from the database

    // Retrieve your custom parameters
    $bg = Widget::getParam('widget_bg', $w->id);
    $limit = Widget::getParam('items_limit', $w->id);

    // Fallback if empty
    $bg = $bg ?: '#ffffff';
    $limit = $limit ?: 5;

    $html = "<div class='my-widget' style='background: {$bg}'>";
    $html .= "<h4>" . $w->title . "</h4>";
    $html .= "<p>Showing latest {$limit} items...</p>";
    $html .= "</div>";

    return $html;
});

docs.title: 4. How It Works (Technical Flow)
docs.text:
1. UI Sync: When an admin selects your widget type, JavaScript triggers an AJAX fetch to WidgetAjax.
  2. AJAX Logic: Params::renderWidget($type) looks for a match in the registered maps and returns the HTML fields.
  3. Persistence: On save, the widgets.control.php controller saves each field into the widgets_param table linked to the widget_id.
  4. Frontend Render: When Widget::show() is called, it executes Widget::content(), which runs the widget_render_{type} hook.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Unique Parameter Names: Prefix your parameter names (e.g., my_mod_bg) to prevent overlap with other widgets.
  • Handle Empty States: Always provide sensible default values in your rendering logic.
  • Cleaner Forms: When your modular parameters are active, the system automatically hides the raw "Content Data" textarea to provide a cleaner UX for users.
  • Icons: Use Bootstrap Icons for professional visual integration in the admin panel.

docs.title: How to Create a Module
docs.text: This guide walks you through building a GeniXCMS module, either automatically using the CLI or manually from scratch.

docs.title: ⚡ Quick Start: Using the CLI
docs.text: The fastest way to scaffold a new module is by using the built-in genix console. This command automatically generates the required directory structure, the index.php entry point with metadata, and a base library class.

docs.title: Generate Scaffolding
docs.text:
In your project root, run:

php genix make:module my-new-plugin

docs.title: Activate the Module
docs.text:
Once generated, you can instantly activate it via the CLI:

php genix module:activate my-new-plugin

docs.title: Module Structure
docs.text:
A module lives in inc/mod/{module-slug}/ and must contain at minimum:

inc/mod/my-module/
├── index.php           ← Required: Module entry point + metadata
├── options.php         ← Optional: Admin settings page
├── inc/
│   └── MyModule.lib.php  ← Main module class
└── layout/
    └── myview.php      ← Frontend template (Latte)

docs.title: Step 1: Create index.php (Entry Point)
docs.text:
The comment block at the top is parsed by the CMS to display the module in the admin panel.

<?php
/**
 * Name: My Module
 * Desc: A brief description of what this module does.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Build: 1.0.0
 * Developer: Your Name
 * URI: https://yoursite.com
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: fa fa-cogs
 */

// Register a PSR-style autoloader for the module's own classes
function loadMyModuleLib($class_name)
{
    Mod::inc($class_name . ".lib", '', dirname(__FILE__) . "/inc/");
}
spl_autoload_register('loadMyModuleLib');

// Instantiate the main module class
new MyModule();

docs.title: Step 2: Create the Module Class (inc/MyModule.lib.php)
docs.text:
The module class constructor hooks into the CMS. The mod_control hook is the key action point for frontend pages.

<?php
class MyModule
{
    public function __construct()
    {
        // Attach to the frontend page hook
        Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['MyModule', 'myPage']);

        // REQUIRED: Register this module's hook ID into Mod::$listMenu.
        // Without this, the URL router will return a 404 for /mod/myPage.html.
        Mod::addMenuList(['myPage' => 'My Module Page']);

        // Modern Admin Menu Registration (Since 2.2.0)
        // Always wrap AdminMenu registration in the 'init' hook to ensure 
        // the core menu is fully booted before adding module items.
        Hooks::attach('init', function() {
            AdminMenu::add([
                'id' => 'mymodule',
                'label' => _('My Module'),
                'icon' => 'bi bi-puzzle',
                'url' => 'index.php?page=mods&mod=my-module',
                'position' => 'main'
            ]);
        });

        // Lifecycle Hooks
        Hooks::attach('my-module_activate', ['MyModule', 'activate']);
        Hooks::attach('my-module_delete', ['MyModule', 'delete']);

        // Optional: Attach to other hooks
        Hooks::attach('footer_load_lib', ['MyModule', 'loadAssets']);
        Hooks::attach('admin_page_dashboard_action', ['MyModule', 'dashboardWidget']);
    }

    /**
     * Activation Hook: Runs when the module is enabled in the admin panel.
     * Ideal for creating database tables.
     */
    public static function activate()
    {
        $sql = "CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS `my_module_table` (
            `id` int(11) NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
            `name` text NOT NULL
        ) ENGINE=InnoDB DEFAULT CHARSET=utf8";
        Db::query($sql);
    }

    /**
     * Delete Hook: Runs when the module is REMOVED/DELETED from the system.
     * Ideal for dropping tables and cleaning up persistent data.
     */
    public static function delete()
    {
        Db::query("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `my_module_table` text");
    }

    /**
     * Frontend page handler.
     * NOTE: Inside hook callbacks, $data is wrapped by Hooks::run() as [$original_data].
     * Use $data[0] to access the actual data array.
     */
    public static function myPage($data)
    {
        // Only execute when this module's URL is active
        if ($data[0]['mod'] !== 'myPage') {
            return;
        }

        $page_data = $data[0]; // Start with the original data

        // Handle form submission
        if (isset($_POST['submit'])) {
            $token = Typo::cleanX($_POST['token']);
            if (!Token::validate($token)) {
                $page_data['alertDanger'][] = _('Invalid security token.');
            } else {
                $message = Typo::cleanX($_POST['message']);
                // Process the form...
                $page_data['alertSuccess'][] = _('Submitted successfully!');
            }
        }

        // REQUIRED: Always return the result of Mod::inc().
        // Hooks::run() collects the return value. If you don't return it,
        // the content will be silently discarded and nothing will render.
        return Mod::inc('myview', $page_data, realpath(__DIR__ . '/../layout/'));
    }

    public static function loadAssets()
    {
        // Recommended: Use the Asset class for dependency & context management
        Asset::register('my-mod-js', 'js', Site::$url . '/inc/mod/my-module/assets/app.js', 'footer', ['jquery'], 20, 'frontend');
        Asset::enqueue('my-mod-js');
    }

    public static function dashboardWidget($data)
    {
        // Output a widget on the admin dashboard
        echo '<div class="col-md-4"><div class="info-box">My Module Stats</div></div>';
    }
}

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Frontend View (layout/myview.php)
docs.text:
Layout files use the Latte template engine.

<section class="container my-5">
    <h1>My Module Page</h1>

    {* Display alerts *}
    {if isset($alertSuccess)}
        <div class="alert alert-success">{$alertSuccess[0]}</div>
    {/if}

    {* Main form *}
    <form method="POST" action="">
        <input type="hidden" name="token" value="{$token}">

        <div class="form-group">
            <label>Your Message</label>
            <textarea name="message" class="form-control" required></textarea>
        </div>

        <button type="submit" name="submit" class="btn btn-primary">Submit</button>
    </form>
</section>

docs.title: Step 4: Create options.php (Admin Settings)
docs.text:
The options.php file is shown in Admin > Modules > [Your Module] > Settings.

<?php
// Save options
if (isset($_POST['mymodule_save'])) {
    $data = [
        'notify_email' => Typo::cleanX($_POST['notify_email']),
    ];
    Options::update('mymodule_options', json_encode($data));
}

$opt = json_decode(Options::get('mymodule_options'), true) ?? [];
?>
<section class="content">
    <h1><i class="fa fa-cogs"></i> My Module Settings</h1>
    <div class="box">
        <div class="box-body">
            <form method="POST">
                <div class="form-group">
                    <label>Notification Email</label>
                    <input type="email" name="notify_email" class="form-control"
                           value="<?= $opt['notify_email'] ?? '' ?>">
                </div>
                <button type="submit" name="mymodule_save" class="btn btn-primary">Save</button>
            </form>
        </div>
    </div>
</section>

docs.title: How Modules Are Loaded
docs.text:
When a user visits /mod/myPage.html (with Smart URL) or ?mod=myPage:

  1. Control::frontend() detects the mod route.
  2. Mod.control.php extracts the module ID from the URL (e.g. myPage).
  3. It checks Mod::$listMenu — populated by Mod::addMenuList() at system init — to confirm the module is valid. If not found, a 404 is returned.
  4. The mod template is rendered, which calls Hooks::run('mod_control', $data).
  5. Your module's callback executes and returns the rendered HTML.
/mod/myPage.html
  → Mod.control.php
  → array_key_exists('myPage', Mod::$listMenu) → valid!
  → render mod template
  → Hooks::run('mod_control', $data)
  → MyModule::myPage($data)          ← $data is [$original_data]
  → return Mod::inc('myview', ...)   ← MUST return!
  → content displayed in template

[!IMPORTANT] Mod::addMenuList() is mandatory for any module that exposes a public frontend URL. The router checks this list to validate requests — a missing entry means a 404 for all visitors.

[!WARNING] Always return the result of Mod::inc() from your hook callback. Hooks::run() collects return values to build the final output. If you omit return, the content is silently discarded and the page body appears blank.

docs.title: Useful Mod Class Methods
docs.text:
// Include a view file from a custom path
Mod::inc('view-name', $data, '/path/to/layout/');

// Register module page in menu manager
Mod::addMenuList(['hookName' => 'Display Label']);

docs.title: Available Hooks for Modules
docs.text: Hook Type Description mod_control Action Frontend page execution point admin_page_dashboard_action Action Add widget to admin dashboard admin_page_top_action Action Output at top of admin page admin_page_bottom_action Action Output at bottom of admin page footer_load_lib Action Add JS/CSS to page footer header_load_meta Action Add tags to HTML <head> post_content_filter Filter Modify post content before display post_submit_add_action Action Fires after a new post is saved user_login_action Action Fires after successful user login

docs.title: Complete Example: Contact Form Module
docs.text:
The built-in Contact Form module demonstrates this full pattern:

inc/mod/contact-form/
├── index.php               — Registers autoloader, instantiates Contact class
├── options.php             — Admin instructions panel
├── inc/
│   └── Contact.lib.php     — Main class: hooks into mod_control,
│                             handles POST, sends email via Mail::send()
└── layout/
    └── contactpage.php     — Latte form template

Key integration points from Contact.lib.php: 

// In constructor — BOTH are required:
Hooks::attach('mod_control', ['Contact', 'contactPage']);
Mod::addMenuList(['contactPage' => 'Contact Us']); // ← enables routing

// In contactPage() — note $data[0] and the return statement:
public static function contactPage($data)
{
    if ($data[0]['mod'] == 'contactPage') {
        // Handle POST, validate, call Mail::send()
        // ...
        // MUST use return so Hooks::run() captures the output:
        return Mod::inc('contactpage', $data[0], realpath(__DIR__.'/../layout/'));
    }
}

docs.title: Installing the Module
docs.text:
1. Place your module folder in inc/mod/my-module/.
  2. Go to Admin > Modules.
  3. Find your module and click Install.
  4. To add a frontend page link, go to Admin > Menus and create a new menu item selecting Mod type with your module's page name.

docs.title: 1. Database Migrations
docs.text:
Don't use Db::query() in your constructor to create tables. Use the Migration System.

  1. Create a migration file in inc/migrations/.
  2. Use Db::query() within the migration's up() method to create your tables.
  3. Users will run php genix migrate to setup your module's database.

docs.title: 2. Access Control List (ACL)
docs.text:
Register custom permissions for your module features in your constructor:

Acl::register('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT', 'Allow users to export my module data');

Then check it before any sensitive action: 

if (Acl::check('MOD_MYMODULE_CAN_EXPORT')) {
    // Perform export...
}

docs.title: 3. API Resources
docs.text:
Expose your module data over the RESTful API:

  1. Create inc/lib/Control/Api/MyResourceApi.class.php.
  2. Your resource will be available at /api/v1/myresource/.

docs.title: 4. Custom Editor Support
docs.text:
If your module provides a new editing experience, register it using the Editor Class:

Editor::register('my_editor', 'My Cool Editor', [self::class, 'initEditor']);

docs.title: 5. Lifecycle Hooks (Installation & Cleanup)
docs.text:
To ensure high performance and clean operations, modules should use lifecycle hooks for database management.

  • {module-slug}_activate: Triggered when the user clicks Activate in the admin panel. Use this to run CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS queries. This ensures queries only run when necessary.
  • {module-slug}_deactivate: Triggered when the module is disabled.
  • {module-slug}_delete: Triggered when the module is completely removed via the "Remove" button. Use this to DROP tables and delete associated files if needed.
// In MyModule.lib.php
public function __construct() {
    Hooks::attach('my-module_activate', [self::class, 'setupDb']);
    Hooks::attach('my-module_delete', [self::class, 'cleanupDb']);
}

public static function setupDb() {
    // Create tables...
}

docs.title: Module Security (CSP & Assets)
docs.text: If your module injects scripts from external CDNs (like Google Maps or Chart.js), you must register these domains in the Content Security Policy whitelist.

docs.title: Registering Trusted Origins
docs.text:
Use the system_security_headers_args hook in your module's constructor:

// In inc/mod/my-module/inc/MyModule.lib.php
public function __construct() {
    Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', [$this, 'extendCSP']);
}

/**
 * Add trusted origins to the global CSP.
 */
public function extendCSP($rules) {
    if (!in_array("https://maps.googleapis.com", $rules['script-src'])) {
        $rules['script-src'][] = "https://maps.googleapis.com";
    }
    return $rules;
}

[!CAUTION] Avoid using http:// for external assets. The core security policy blocks insecure sources by default to prevent mixed content vulnerabilities.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hooks Class — All available hooks.
  • Mod Class — Module utilities.
  • Mail Class — Send emails from modules.
  • Options Class — Store and retrieve module settings.
  • Token Class — CSRF protection for module forms.
  • Control Layer Reference — How module routes are dispatched.
  • Model Class — Base ORM class.

docs.title: Theme Development Guide
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a powerful, decoupled theme engine that allows you to craft high-performance frontend experiences. This guide provides a step-by-step walkthrough for building a custom theme from the ground up, leveraging the Latte templating engine and the integrated Hooks system.

docs.title: ⚡ Quick Start: Using the CLI
docs.text: The fastest way to start a new theme is by using the built-in genix console. This command automatically generates the required directory and all boilerplate files (header.php, footer.php, index.php, etc.).

docs.title: Generate Scaffolding
docs.text:
In your project root, run:

php genix make:theme my-awesome-theme

docs.title: Activate the Theme
docs.text:
Once generated, you can instantly activate it via the CLI:

php genix theme:activate my-awesome-theme

docs.title: 🏗️ Theme Architecture
docs.text: Themes are located in inc/themes/{your-theme-name}/. A well-structured theme follows this standardized file layout:

docs.title: 📂 Core Required Files
docs.text: File Role themeinfo.php Metadata: Defines theme name, version, and developer info. header.php Head Section: Contains <head>, meta tags, and top navigation. footer.php Tail Section: Contains footer layout and closing tags. index.php Homepage: The default template for the root URL. single.php Post View: The template for individual blog posts. css/style.css Styling: The default stylesheet loaded automatically by OptionsBuilder.

docs.title: 📂 Logic & Customization
docs.text:
• function.php: Optional logic file to register hooks, load assets, and define theme classes.
  • options.php: Optional admin panel for theme-specific settings.

docs.title: 🚀 Step 1: Defining Theme Metadata (themeinfo.php)
docs.text:
GeniXCMS parses the comment block in this file to display theme information in the administration dashboard.

<?php
/*
 * Name: Modern Nexus
 * Desc: A clean, performance-optimized theme for GeniXCMS 2.0.
 * Version: 1.0.0
 * Developer: GeniXCMS Team
 * URI: https://genixcms.web.id
 * License: MIT License
 * Icon: bi bi-palette
 */

docs.title: 🎨 Step 2: The Shell (header.php & footer.php)
docs.text: GeniXCMS uses Latte, which means variables are accessible via {$variable} and logic via {if} or {foreach}.

docs.title: header.php
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="{$website_lang}">
<head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8">
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
    <title>{$sitetitle|noescape} - {$site_name}</title>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>
<body>
<nav class="navbar navbar-expand-lg">
    <div class="container">
        <a class="navbar-brand" href="{$site_url}">{$site_logo|noescape}</a>
        {Menus::getMenu('main')|noescape}
    </div>
</nav>

docs.title: footer.php
docs.text:
<footer class="bg-dark text-white py-4 mt-5">
    <div class="container text-center">
        {$site_footer|noescape}
    </div>
</footer>
{* Critical: Required to load system & module scripts *}
{Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>
</html>

[!IMPORTANT] Don't Forget the Hooks: Always include {Hooks::run('footer_load_lib')|noescape} before </body>. Without it, core features like AJAX, popups, and module scripts will fail.

docs.title: 📝 Step 3: Content Templates (index.php)
docs.text:
The homepage template receives the $posts array automatically.

<main class="container py-5">
    <div class="row">
        {foreach $posts as $post}
        <div class="col-md-4 mb-4">
            <div class="card h-100 shadow-sm">
                <div class="card-body">
                    <h2 class="h5">
                        <a href="{Url::post($post->id)}">{$post->title|noescape}</a>
                    </h2>
                    <p class="text-muted small">
                         By {$post->author} | {Date::format($post->date)|noescape}
                    </p>
                    <p class="card-text">
                        {Posts::excerpt($post->content, 120)|noescape}
                    </p>
                </div>
            </div>
        </div>
        {/foreach}
    </div>
    <div class="d-flex justify-content-center mt-4">
        {$paging|noescape}
    </div>
</main>

docs.title: 🔧 Step 4: Theme Logic (function.php)
docs.text:
Use this file to register assets and hooks. It's best practice to wrap your logic in a Theme Class.

<?php
class NexusTheme {
    public function __construct() {
        // Register all theme-specific assets
        $this->registerAssets();

        // Define widget areas for this theme
        Widget::addLocation('nexus_header_top', 'Top Header Area');
        Widget::addLocation('nexus_sidebar_left', 'Left Sidebar');
    }

    private function registerAssets() {
        // Enqueue pre-defined system assets (jQuery is core)
        Asset::enqueue(['bootstrap-css', 'bootstrap-js', 'bootstrap-icons']);

        // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific CSS (loads in header by default)
        Asset::register('nexus-style', 'css', Url::theme() . '/assets/css/style.css', 'header');
        Asset::enqueue('nexus-style');

        // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific JS (loads in footer)
        Asset::register('nexus-script', 'js', Url::theme() . '/assets/js/theme.js', 'footer', ['jquery']);
        Asset::enqueue('nexus-script');
    }
}
new NexusTheme();

docs.title: 🧩 Step 5: Rendering Widgets
docs.text:
Once you've registered your custom widget locations in function.php, users will be able to place blocks there via the GeniXCMS Admin Dashboard (Appearance > Widgets).

To render these dynamic blocks into your theme designs, use Widget::show():

<div class="row">
    <!-- Left Sidebar pulling widgets assigned to 'nexus_sidebar_left' -->
    <aside class="col-md-3">
        {Widget::show('nexus_sidebar_left')|noescape}
    </aside>

    <!-- Main Content Area -->
    <main class="col-md-9">
        ...
    </main>
</div>

docs.title: 🛠️ Latte Syntax Quick Reference
docs.text: Action Syntax Print Variable {$var \| noescape} (Escaping is automatic by default) Include File {include 'sidebar.latte'} Looping {foreach $items as $item} ... {/foreach} Conditional {if $count > 0} ... {elseif $count == 0} ... {/if} Editor Setup {Theme::editor('full', '300') | noescape} PHP Method {ClassName::method($params) | noescape}

docs.title: 🔒 Handling Security (CSP)
docs.text:
If your theme loads external scripts from a third-party CDN, you must whitelist the domain using the system_security_headers_args hook:

Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', function($rules) {
    // Whitelist a new CDN for scripts
    $rules['script-src'][] = "https://cdn.example.com";
    return $rules;
});

docs.title: ⚙️ Step 6: Theme Options & Dynamic CSS
docs.text: If you want to offer an administrative panel for users to customize your theme (colors, typography, etc.), GeniXCMS provides the OptionsBuilder engine. It creates a robust UI in the backend and dynamically generates CSS variables for the frontend.

docs.title: 1. Registering the Admin Menu
docs.text:
Add a child menu item in your function.php constructor so users can access your theme's options:

AdminMenu::addChild('themes', [
    'label'  => _('Nexus Options'),
    'url'    => 'index.php?page=themes&view=options',
    'icon'   => 'bi bi-sliders',
    'access' => 0,
]);

docs.title: 2. Rendering Dynamic CSS
docs.text:
To output the customized styles into the <head>, hook into header_load_lib.

Critical Rule: You must return the CSS string instead of echoing it, to ensure it gets captured correctly by Site::loadLibHeader(). If your theme provides its own CSS file (e.g., assets/css/blog.css) and you don't want OptionsBuilder to auto-inject a <link> to style.css, pass 'loadStyleCss' => false.

Hooks::attach('header_load_lib', [__CLASS__, 'loadThemeCSS']);

public static function loadThemeCSS() {
    $css = '';

    // Generate dynamic CSS based on saved options
    if (class_exists('OptionsBuilder')) {
        $css .= OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS(self::$opt, [
            'themeUrl' => Url::theme(),
            'minify' => true,
            'loadStyleCss' => false // Set to false if you manually load your CSS files
        ]);
    }

    // Add any custom inline CSS here
    $css .= "<style>\n";
    $css .= "  :root { --nexus-color: " . (self::$opt['custom_color'] ?? '#000') . "; }\n";
    $css .= "</style>\n";

    // Return the string to keep the HTML structured properly
    return $css;
}

docs.title: 📱 Step 7: Dynamic Page Layouts
docs.text: GeniXCMS 2.1.1+ introduces a robust Dynamic Page Layout system. This feature allows theme developers to provide multiple design options for a single page (e.g., Landing Page, Fullwidth, Sidebar-Left, or Blank) which the end-user can select via a dropdown in the Admin Dashboard.

docs.title: 📂 Pro-Developer Naming Conventions
docs.text:
The system uses a strict naming pattern to automatically discover and link templates. All files must reside in your theme's root directory:

Template Type File Pattern Role Main View layout-{slug}.latte Replaces the entire page.latte content area. Custom Header header-{slug}.latte Replaces header.latte when this layout is active. Custom Footer footer-{slug}.latte Replaces footer.latte when this layout is active.

[!NOTE] {slug} is the unique identifier you choose (e.g., landing, blank, fullwidth).

docs.title: 🏗️ Creating a Registerable Layout
docs.text:
To make your layout appear in the Admin Panel with a human-readable name, add a specially formatted comment on the first line of your layout-{slug}.latte file.

Example: layout-landing.latte 

{* Layout: Premium Landing Page *}
<div class="landing-hero bg-primary text-white p-5">
    <h1 class="display-1">{$title}</h1>
    <div class="lead">{$content|noescape}</div>
</div>
If no comment is found, GeniXCMS will generate a name from the slug (e.g., "landing" becomes "Landing").

docs.title: 🔗 Technical Execution & Lookup Order
docs.text:
When a user visits a page assigned to a custom layout (e.g., slug = "blank"), the BaseControl::render() engine follows this precise lookup priority:

  1. Header Search:
    • Look for header-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard header.latte.
  2. View Search:
    • Look for layout-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard page.latte.
  3. Footer Search:
    • Look for footer-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard footer.latte.

docs.title: 💡 Use Case: The "Blank" Workspace
docs.text:
Commonly used for pages inside iframes or landing pages that shouldn't have site navigation.

1. Create header-blank.latte (Minimalist head section): 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>
<body class="bg-white">

2. Create layout-blank.latte (Just the content): 

{* Layout: Clean Workspace *}
<main class="container py-3">
    {$content|noescape}
</main>

3. Create footer-blank.latte (Minimalist scripts): 

{Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 🛠️ Variable Access
docs.text:
Any custom layout file has full access to the standard GeniXCMS data object, including: }

private function registerAssets() {
    // Enqueue pre-defined system assets (jQuery is core)
    Asset::enqueue(['bootstrap-css', 'bootstrap-js', 'bootstrap-icons']);

    // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific CSS (loads in header by default)
    Asset::register('nexus-style', 'css', Url::theme() . '/assets/css/style.css', 'header');
    Asset::enqueue('nexus-style');

    // Register & Enqueue Theme-specific JS (loads in footer)
    Asset::register('nexus-script', 'js', Url::theme() . '/assets/js/theme.js', 'footer', ['jquery']);
    Asset::enqueue('nexus-script');
}

} new NexusTheme(); ```

docs.title: 🧩 Step 5: Rendering Widgets
docs.text: Once you've registered your custom widget locations in function.php, users will be able to place blocks there via the GeniXCMS Admin Dashboard (Appearance > Widgets). To render these dynamic blocks into your theme designs, use Widget::show(): html <div class="row"> <!-- Left Sidebar pulling widgets assigned to 'nexus_sidebar_left' --> <aside class="col-md-3"> {Widget::show('nexus_sidebar_left')|noescape} </aside> <!-- Main Content Area --> <main class="col-md-9"> ... </main> </div>

docs.title: 🛠️ Latte Syntax Quick Reference
docs.text: Action Syntax Print Variable {$var \| noescape} (Escaping is automatic by default) Include File {include 'sidebar.latte'} Looping {foreach $items as $item} ... {/foreach} Conditional {if $count > 0} ... {elseif $count == 0} ... {/if} Editor Setup {Theme::editor('full', '300') | noescape} PHP Method {ClassName::method($params) | noescape}

If your theme loads external scripts from a third-party CDN, you must whitelist the domain using the system_security_headers_args hook:

Hooks::attach('system_security_headers_args', function($rules) {
    // Whitelist a new CDN for scripts
    $rules['script-src'][] = "https://cdn.example.com";
    return $rules;
});

Critical Rule: You must return the CSS string instead of echoing it, to ensure it gets captured correctly by Site::loadLibHeader(). If your theme provides its own CSS file (e.g., assets/css/blog.css) and you don't want OptionsBuilder to auto-inject a <link> to style.css, pass 'loadStyleCss' => false.

        $css .= OptionsBuilder::generateFrontendCSS(self::$opt, [
            'themeUrl' => Url::theme(),

Example: layout-landing.latte

<div class="landing-hero bg-primary text-white p-5">
    <h1 class="display-1">{$title}</h1>
    <div class="lead">{$content|noescape}</div>

docs.title: 🔗 Technical Execution & Lookup Order
docs.text:
When a user visits a page assigned to a custom layout (e.g., slug = "blank"), the BaseControl::render() engine follows this precise lookup priority:

  1. Header Search:
    • Look for header-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard header.latte.
  2. View Search:
    • Look for layout-blank.latte (or .php).

  3. Footer Search:
    • Look for footer-blank.latte (or .php).
    • Fallback: Use the standard footer.latte.

docs.title: 💡 Use Case: The "Blank" Workspace
docs.text:
Commonly used for pages inside iframes or landing pages that shouldn't have site navigation.

1. Create header-blank.latte (Minimalist head section): 

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
    {$site_meta|noescape}
    {Site::loadLibHeader()|noescape}
</head>
<body class="bg-white">

2. Create layout-blank.latte (Just the content): 

{* Layout: Clean Workspace *}
<main class="container py-3">
    {$content|noescape}
</main>

3. Create footer-blank.latte (Minimalist scripts): 

{Site::loadLibFooter()|noescape}
</body>
</html>

docs.title: 🛠️ Variable Access
docs.text: Any custom layout file has full access to the standard GeniXCMS data object, including: - {$title}: The page title. - {$content}: The main content (rendered HTML). - {$data['post']}: The full post object. - {$site_name}, {$site_url}, etc.

docs.title: 🛡️ Theme Lifecycle Hooks (Activate & Delete)
docs.text:
Since version 2.2.1, themes can use lifecycle hooks to perform setup or cleanup tasks (e.g., creating tables or deleting temporary assets).

  • {theme-slug}_activate: Runs when a user activates the theme. Use this for initial setup.
  • {theme-slug}_delete: Runs just before the theme is removed from disk. Use this to clean up persistent data.
// In inc/themes/my-theme/function.php
class MyTheme {
    public function __construct() {
        Hooks::attach('my-theme_activate', [self::class, 'install']);
        Hooks::attach('my-theme_delete',   [self::class, 'cleanup']);
    }

    public static function install() {
        // Create custom tables if needed...
    }

    public static function cleanup() {
        // Remove data when theme is deleted...
    }
}

[!TIP] Pro Tip: Use the Vite Helper to integrate modern tools like Tailwind CSS and SCSS into your theme development workflow.

docs.title: Creating RESTful API Services
docs.text: GeniXCMS features a modular API dispatching system that allows any module to expose its own RESTful endpoints without modifying the core system. The system automatically routes requests based on class naming conventions.

docs.title: 🏗️ The API Architecture
docs.text:
GeniXCMS API follows a standard versioned structure: https://your-site.com/api/v1/{resource}/{identifier}

When a request is made to this URL, the Api class attempts to find and instantiate a handler class named {Resource}Api.

docs.title: 🛠️ Registering a Custom API Service
docs.text: To create an API service for your module, you simply need to define a class that follows the naming convention.

docs.title: 1. Define the API Class
docs.text:
Create a class in your module (e.g., in YourModule.lib.php or directly in init hook) named after your resource plus the Api suffix.

class MarketplaceApi
{
    // If true, no API Key / Token is required for GET requests
    public $is_public = true;

    /**
     * Handle GET requests
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/ OR /api/v1/marketplace/123
     */
    public function index($id = null)
    {
        if ($id) {
            $data = Posts::fetch(['id' => $id, 'type' => 'marketplace']);
            return Api::success($data, 'Item retrieved');
        } else {
            $data = Posts::lists(['type' => 'marketplace', 'num' => 10]);
            return Api::success($data, 'List of items');
        }
    }

    /**
     * Handle POST requests (Create)
     * URL: /api/v1/marketplace/
     */
    public function submit()
    {
        // Access JSON input
        $input = json_decode(file_get_contents('php://input'), true);

        // Process logic...

        return Api::success($input, 'Item created successfully');
    }

    /**
     * Handle PUT/PATCH requests (Update)
